Although the name may be sinister, the robes and the people who wear them are not automatically evil. 

The first set of dark mage robes were so named because the technique to craft them required cloth that had been dyed by a unique alchemical mixture. As the years passed and Guthix introduced runes, the mixture was replaced with subtle enchantments, though the appearance was kept the same for tradition’s sake. 

Naturally, many practitioners of magic preferred this armour because of its darker aesthetic. Indeed, many rogue “dark wizards” would don this armour and use magic to engage in sinister undertakings, earning this armour’s moniker a more sinister meaning.

The knights of Saradomin had many names over the years. White Knights. Temple Knights. In Ashenfall they were called Paladins. Brave, and faithful, members of noble families that took up the sword.

They were the second, third and fourth children of Brynmoor’s “nobility” – a title given to those families that were most loyal to the priesthood, and the most obedient were raised the highest.

This helm was a symbol of pride to the respectable families of Brynmoor. A sign that the bearer had been chosen to stand as the guardian of Saradominist faith for all of Brynmoor to see. It earned them favour, it earned the status, it earned them the ire of those who followed a different faith.

During the final years of Bramblemead corruption had run rampant. Nobles took more than their share and the priests who watched over them turned a blind eye in exchange for their own share of glittering coin. Those who did not possess noble blood, found that their lives became harder, their earnings became smaller and joy became a much more fleeting feeling than it had done before.

One woman decided to change that. She would attack trader caravans marked with goods for the castle, taking from their purses of coin. Tax collectors would also feel the sting of her arrows, surrendering their wealth to the woman clad in green. She was given the name Nightingale, because of the whistling noise her arrows made as they flew through the air, an almost musical tone that sent fear through her targets.

Villagers would then wake in the morning following an attack, to find purses of coin in flowerboxes, window shelves, or resting neatly under the fattest, laziest, cats.

Reinforced leather, designed to withstand powerful attacks. Layers of stiffened leather folded together to strengthen one another, turning singular sheets into a powerful defensive force. 

While metal is the preferred material for armour, leather has its charm. Its wearers boast greater agility and find it easier to hide the sounds of their movements than they can in metal gear. But its most important benefit is the relative cheapness in construction.

Those who joining the Fellhollow military, who were not from the more noble bloodlines, would join the force known as the hoplites. This force of warrior farmers named themselves after the hoplite forces of Hallow, legendary forces spoken of in some of the Saradominist text.

They were disciplined in a way that earned them great respect. Each of them acting as the shield for the person next to them and a spear for the enemy in front. They wore armour reflecting some of the style of Hallow, with plateskirts rather than sturdy grieves, allowing for more freedom of movement, a trait they took great advantage of.

The last hunter in Fellhollow hid in the trees for as long as he could. Beneath him the dead shambled and stumbled and clawed at the bark, yet he remained in the trees safely out of reach.

He fed himself on nuts and berries, stealing form the treasure troves of squirrels and birds, occasionally risking a trip to the ground to hunt a passing animal. He gave up on cooking the meat he hunted, as he could not stay on the ground long enough before the dead rose up to face him, so he tore into the carcasses of deer, sheep and rats, trying his best not to vomit.

He barely noticed when his appetite changed, since he was so used raw flesh and the sickly cold taste of blood pooling in his mouth. So, when the withering began to take him, he hardly noticed.

Sometimes, if you’re quick enough, you may catch a glimpse of a skeleton in the tree branches. Even in death he fears the monsters on the ground.

Poor old Noggin found no peace in death, because even the gods forgot him. When he reached the River Noumenon, there was no guide to usher him through to the Afterlife. No radiant light to beckon him forward into the loving arms of those he'd lost. instead, there was silence and it went on and on. 

Always quiet and forgettable, the universe simply failed to recall him. His name was recorded in no books of the dead. The various creatures that prey on lost souls, never once thought he seemed like a pleasant meal. Instead they did nothing, except to leave him to his wandering, forgotten and alone. 

Then four people went digging where they shouldn't, and that made Noggin mad. Forgettable as he was, he had always made a point of preserving the status quo and to see these four ruffians blindly digging into the soil that had never done anyone a disservice, why that was unforgivable – he couldn't let it stand. So he crept into their camp, and found the treasure they coveted, and secreted himself within the gold and silver, waiting to be found. Found he was, for when they opened the chest they saw no treasure, no jewels, no trinkets of worth – because Noggin had eaten all of them, swallowed the pieces down into the void – they found only a ghostly sackcloth head glaring back at them, with nothing but hate in its eyes. 

This is that piece of sackcloth, and it's not happy to see you.

Not all druids follow the notion that one must live a life of peaceful coexistence. After all, the animal kingdom is full of conflict and conquest, predator and prey, as well as battles for dominance. Some druids feel, therefore, that to truly emulate nature one must do so fully, including tooth and claw. The problem there is that druids are, by nature, generally on the squishier side and so they needed armour that would protect their vital areas better than their normal robes.

On Ashenfall, it was the druid Pikenmix who first tried to break out of the peace-loving mould. They learned magic that would allow them to hurl flame from their fingertips hoping it might be enough. Yet, as the enemies got too close, she soon learned that she needed stronger defence than her meagre hood would allow.

It was when she was lost in thought, that she found the solution – by walking straight into a tree. Her nose broke in several places and her entire body screamed in pain, yet the tree remained unaffected by her violent interaction.

So it was that she crafted herself a helm made from the very same tree. Partially in revenge, but mostly because she knew how strong the bark could be.

It was late in the development of Fellhollow that the people needed armour. They made do with old hand me down leather and bronze gear, brought with them from Bramblemead, for the most part, because what need was there for armour when you were protected by dragons.

Kuldra would put a stop to that mindset in the end, but it was the Children of Shadows who forged the first steel gear in Fellhollow. One member, in particular, Sadia Vulketra, began to feel that the order lacked the strength it may one day need to defend against the Saradominist forces.

There was no war. No conflict like that in Emberwood, indeed the four major faiths got along rather well with one another. Yet, the history books told Sadia exactly what the priests of Saradomin would do to the worshippers of the god of chaos.

She forged a helm first, with the assistance of one of her many lovers, a blacksmith Amalgamated called Vesk. The first helmet was styled with great curling horns, stained black by the forge’s flames.

Naturally, this style didn’t prove popular with the rest of the village, as they began crafting steel helms of their own, favouring a more simplistic approach, and a feathered plume, to look fancy.

Reinforced leather, with metal plates woven throughout. The plates rest underneath leather coverings, with metal studs to hold them in place, giving the armour its name. Favoured by scouts and hunters for the mixture of agility and defence. 

Most Wild Knights favoured studded leather armour over the more durable metal plate. Moving through the forests required as much grace as guts, and stealth is always a hunter’s friend. 

When they were brought into the war against the garou, the leather armour proved less popular at first. While durable and agile, leather has a strong and distinctive smell. The garou’s olfactory senses were much stronger than humans were prepared for, capable of tracking down the leathery smell from a disturbing range. Wind magic helped to keep the scent away from them, but it wasn’t enough to eradicate it.

Sir Percival Lateef was a proud man, assured of his own prowess, character and moral standing. On the mainland, he would train the other knights in swordsmanship, as well as host several long and laborious lectures on his supposed life as an explorer. He had travelled across the lands, to the frozen polar regions, and the jungles of Karamja, but no one ever seemed particularly interested in his stories.

Tired of being ignored and teased for his exploits, he took a boat and sailed it north of the mainland into regions unknown. His boat was destroyed. His crew left for dead, and he found himself on a unknown beach of strange, unnatural hues. The first human on Ashenfall for centuries, he was finally the explorer he dreamed himself to be.

The Wild Knights were known for their ability to scout the wild areas of Ashenfall. They could walk where no one else dared to tread, and they were masters of the hunt, putting even the garou to shame.

Their archers wore sturdy leathers, using carefully laid folds to shield them against harm. They would break easily, but the archers made it part of their ritual to maintain them regularly. Each day they would wake and tend to the leather, making their armour seem brand new with each morning.

The helm is designed to focus the wearer’s vision, so that their target receives all their attention. Distractions are cut out of the picture. Everything exterior to their quarry is removed from view. The archer’s sight is trained on their victim and, so the rumours claimed, they never missed their marks.

These blue robes are the signature clothing worn by every respectable mage. Apprentices look forward to the day they first wear these, as it shows they have graduated to full wizard status. The wizard's tower, in particular, uses these robes as a uniform, signifying their magical power and fealty to the god of order, Saradomin.

On Ashenfall, it was rare for those wearing these robes to be seen outside the wizard's guild. The structure on the hillside was home to strange practices and even stranger experiments. Powerful spells were studied and unleashed across an already magically problematic countryside.

The White Knights, often summoned to address the perils that the wizard's guild inadvertently unleashed, were a frequent presence. Their duties ranged from quelling peculiar weather phenomena to managing the 'accidental' summoning of demons. To maintain control, a contingent of knights established a permanent residence within the guild, their vigilant eyes ever watchful.

It's hard to look at what the Zamorakian Mages have become. They were once a noble order, committed to chaos, demonology and destruction. You would fear to be in their presence, particularly that of their leader, Alric. Now, you fear them for wholly different reasons. The Reach has twisted them into something bestial. 

The downfall was sudden. They would not admit it, but the magi had lost control of their demons. Their reaction was the same as always: to deny and deflect. If you accused the magi of summoning demons outside of sanctioned areas, they would smirk and give a carousel of different answers: you are lying; you are mistaken; you are cowardly; you are to be eaten by demons. 

Something terrible was bound to happen; it was a question of when. An infernal demon materialised during a Shadow Knight training session. The recruits were memorising guard rotations, so only their teacher was armed. It was a massacre. 

Alric and Lord Rasmodel locked themselves in the war room to argue. None were to enter. Only the two of them know what was said - oh, to be a raven, watching - but everyone knows the result. 

The Dreaming Stone, and the allocation of power that came from it, would be managed solely by Lord Rasmodel. The demon portal would have its power rationed.

Alric and his magi had their wings clipped.

You brought simple cloth trousers with you from the mainland. They barely qualify as armour, but they’re better than no armour at all. 

These clothes have seen you across the oceans, keeping you warm in the cold sea breeze. Now starched with sea salt and weathered by labouring away on the ship deck, these clothes are a testament to how far you’ve come. This is the furthest you’ve ever been from home, and perhaps it’s a chance to craft a new one.

It's hard not to look at the Black Knight armour and marvel. The Black Knights on Ashenfall have completely different origins from that of the mainland. They have no direct connection to the Kinshra, and are instead evolutions of the Shadow Knights. Two connected but ultimately different factions, yet they converge on similar armours. I suppose that's chaos for you. Stare at it long enough and you spot patterns.

The Children of Shadows learned to be wary of Lord Rasmodel in the mornings. He was cruel before noon, fair in the afternoons, and stern all day. Those who were close to him knew the reasoning: Lord Rasmodel slept poorly, and would complain that his dreams kept him up. He was confused by them: they talked in words that he didn't recognise. He found their voices to be dull and interminable, like being locked in a room with a droning aristocrat. 

The dreams made him impatient and quick to bore. His relationship with Alric, Archmage, deteriorated. Rasmodel even suspected that Alric was behind his restless nights. 

One morning, Rasmodel emerged with a different kind of impatience. He was resolved that something had to change. Fellhollow, Imaru and Xikotal were not to be trusted. A new order of knights were to be created, and he was to lead them. At their centre would be his bodyguards, loyal and unquestioning. 

The Shadow Knights were born, and time would harden them into the Black Knights.

The Shadow Knights, or Black Knights, as they would come to be known, were mostly melee and ranged fighters. When magic was needed, they would enlist Alric's magi, but that secondment was always short. The melee knights were mostly posted in the grounds, but the ranged knights had a different role: they were stationed around the demon gate.

Lord Rasmodel's greatest challenge came from within. It did not carry a sword or crossbow. 

The Shadow Knight numbers were dropping. It was impossible to resign from the order - that was written in the charter - but more and more of the knights were falling into madness. If it were physical wounds, Lord Rasmodel could have done something about the epidemic. He could amputate the problem with a single swing of his sword. But his enemy was intangible. 

He decided to visit those on the precipice of madness. Most were unintelligible, but some talked of food not satisfying them anymore. Others called out to the Dreaming Stone like it was their mother, as if it would save them. In many cases, it sounded like the afflicted were arguing with themselves: two enemies unleashing an enmity. 

One of the mindless disquieted Lord Rasmodel more than the others. She was an afflicted soldier that he knew from long ago. She rambled in a voice that mimicked the droning of a bored aristocrat. Lord Rasmodel knew that voice well. He stumbled from the bedside of the soldier and took no other visitors that day.

Blue dragons are not native to Dowdun Reach, because blue dragons are not native anywhere. They are creations of the dragonkin, and this particular strain is Lacrussa's work. Lacrussa did not consider them failures, but she did not consider them successes, either. They were stepping stones: and, as such, she let them rest on Dowdun Reach.

Avisk maneuvered the multi-faceted prism within the blue dragon's mind. He observed it using his mastery of air, but no permutation would focus the blue dragon's thoughts, no orientation would align the chaos. He resolved to break parts of that giant stone, but its power was diminished with each snap and shatter. He crumpled to the floor of the blue dragon's mind-space in frustration. 

The breakthrough was borne from that frustration. Just as he would move into the consciousness of a living being, he moved into the consciousness of the stone. Perhaps he forgot where he was. Against all his expectations, he was able to step into the stone itself, as if it itself had a consciousness. There was a second layer to the blue dragon's mind.

It is impossible to comprehend what Avisk saw there. But he was forever changed. He no longer cared for healing the blue dragons of their crystalline blockage, of restoring them to sapient thought. Instead, he wished to recreate the prism, the crystal, in the corporeal world.

Bronze was once a secret held by the ancient dwarves. It is a more robust metal forged from an alliance of two others. 

As the Saradomin faith became more popular, the bronze-clad inquisition enforced Saradomin’s laws, even when they conflicted with the Guthixian faith. It was a slow change, but they went from clearing shrines of monsters to clearing shrines of the Guthixian heresy. 

When the druids finally rebelled, the bronze-clad enforcers were called against them, not the White Knights. The cruel irony of being arrested by the force created to protect them.

Although the name may be sinister, the robes and the people who wear them are not automatically evil. 

The most infamous dark wizards became a fairytale for the people of Brynmoor, a cautionary tale about greed and the desire for power. They sought power in the darkest of places, and it turned them into something monstrous. 

They appeared in the village in the dead of night as twisted versions of themselves—mindless creatures infused with a shadowy essence. Those who found themselves in their path had their lives ripped out of them until nought but an empty husk remained. 

It took all the visiting Wild Knights to take them down. Arrow after arrow, spell after spell, the task seemed almost insurmountable until the light of the sun made the creatures vulnerable. In their death throws, it was as if some horrible chill fell across the world as if a door somewhere had been opened into somewhere it shouldn’t.

The dragonkin of Ashenfall all reveled in power, gaining elemental mastery from the strange anima of the land. Although their kind tended to ignore magic, in favour of alchemy and science, the dragonkin of Ashenfall enjoyed their time as sorcerer kings.

They were robes that empowered their magic, using alchemical techniques to create cloth that would resist their elemental powers. Fire would not burn so easily, cold would not damage the threads and lightning could be turned aside with a flick of a cloak. Though these techniques are lost to time, the echoes of their powerful resistance remains.

Lacrussa was the only dragonkin to resist the alure of magic. She insisted that science was the greater power, capable of almost anything with time and study. Lacrussa wore the robes as an a small act of rebellion, claiming that magic would always be overcome in the end.

Unlike her companions, Lacrussa refused to decorate her robes. She took no joy in precious stones, or shining metals. She refused to embroider designs across the cloak. She did, however, use alchemical techniques to enhance the defensive power of the robes. Something the other dragonkin soon came to copy, proving, in her mind, the superiority of science.

Wear the skin of your enemies, so that they know to fear you! This is the ranger way, to dress themselves in the outer layers of weaker dragons so that they larger dragons know not to mess with them.

The funeral of Bilar Amaris was not a particularly popular one. A few fellow hunters stopped by to pay the briefest of respects, and a drunk great uncle turned up and got the name wrong. His death would have passed into complete obscurity, except for the clothes he was wearing when he died.

Despite the mauled condition of his corpse, the villagers that found him noticed the surprisingly supple and yet sturdy armour. Barely a scratch covered the dragonhide, and they remarked that if only he’d covered the neck a bit better, then he might not have ended up in the state he did.

The villagers brought the armour back and leatherworkers set to work on repeating it and improving it. Soon the best hunters in Emberwood would be wearing green dragonhide armour, and matched with better common sense, it kept them alive much longer.

Hardened leather is a sturdier defence, able to turn away more powerful attacks. Favoured by hunters and rangers, who like the defence it offers without the reduction in mobility brought on by sturdier armour.

In poor weather, particularly rain and cold, the armour would create a distinctive creaking noise. Barely audible for the most part, but in the silence of night it felt like a deafening roar. Wild Knights made up for the sound of their armour, as they crept through the streets to rescue Amalgamated, by using simple spells to distract people.

They called animal spirits, notably crows, ravens and magpies, which would caw angrily at anyone nearby. This would earn the term “dark tidings”, referring to whenever the Wild Knights were seen near town – in reference to the magpie spirits they would summon.

Blacksmiths everywhere favour iron as a material to forge with. It is strong, durable, and malleable when hot and yet rigid when cool. It has been used to create weapons and unbreakable containers. Yet, it is most popular when turned into protection for those forced to ride into battle.

From the outset, the search for iron ignited a fierce conflict between the humans and the Garou. Each incursion escalated the tension, pushing both sides perilously close to the brink of war. However, the situation reached a critical juncture when Bandos dispatched an emissary to the villagers of Brynmoor, offering a solution to their Garou predicament.

The Saradominist priests met with emissaries of Bandos and came to an alliance. The humans would claim the mines of Ghornfell, as long as Bandos could claim the head of the Garou goddess, Lougrim.
A win-win, as far as the priests were concerned.

With a distinct aroma, leather armour became very popular with warriors and hunters, as well as a few villagers, but that’s another story. Leather, when properly layered and cured, can turn away both the claws of a beast and the blades of an assailant.

Brynmoor villagers favoured simpler styles, which were easy to produce and focused on defence. When traversing the wild forests of Ashenfall, they quickly found that more elaborate designs got in the way. It was better to keep things simple so that they might be more easily repaired than to choose style over function.

Still, it was common for villagers to add small adornments to their leather armour to make them stand out. Patches made to resemble animals were common practices, though flowers were also popular.

Gentle perfumes were also favoured to help limit the leather’s distinctive smell.

A cousin to the better-known Boots of Lightness, popular on the Gielinor mainland. These leggings were created by Shana, a Bramblemead villager who hated the cold. While the boots were functional, and useful, making it easier to carry things round the village, she always felt the winds chill no matter what she wore.

Her solution was simple. Since enchantment created an excess of energy, at least when she did it – she was never going to become a wizard – she laced the leggings with the lightness charm.

The effect was twofold. Firstly, she could carry things around the town as easily as if she were wearing the boots. Secondly, the enchantment kept her legs nice and warm.

Fortunately, the designs for these leggings improved over the years, removing the overheating problem that Shana had been so fond of. Although she was doubtless less fond of it after her house caught fire.

Progress demands sacrifice.

Prized for its lightweight and nimble nature, linen armour finds favour among apprentice wizards and those who value agility. Composed of layered cloth, it forms a rudimentary defence capable of withstanding minor assaults. 

This simple armour would remain popular with apprentice wizards long into the third age, falling out of fashion only when faced with something more dangerous than a goblin.

The beastmen further north of Brynmoor used weapons far more vicious than a goblin might wield. They tore through linen armour as if it were nothing, and the diminished enchantments of the acolytes did nothing to shield the blows.

Apprentice wizards died in droves, and the armour fell out of favour.

Would Dowdun Reach be standing without mithril? At the very least, it would be standing elsewhere. And since 'elsewhere' would put it in the path of dragons, the Withering and Saradominists at various points on Ashenfall's timeline, you would have to surmise that no, Dowdun Reach would not exist.

There was a time, before the Withering, when a choice was made. The Children of Shadows became paranoid about the prospect of attack and betrayal. It did not seem to matter that they were guarded by dragons who manufactured peace. Dark whispers moved them to act.

Their lord, a man by the name of Rasmodel, prepared an edict whereby a faction would form. This would be the Shadow Knights, and the knights would enforce the will of Rasmodel. 

An aspiring knight needs weapons and armour, which led to Rasmodel's choice. Were the knights to wear mithril, the metal that now freely flowed from the mines of Dowdun? It was strong enough, but mithril's value was in its perceived rarity. Or were they to import a different metal, one that would be costly and awkward to source - particularly for a force that wished to remain inconspicuous?

In the end, the choice was made for Rasmodel. The dragon, Xikotal, had a gift. In one claw, he had coal, in the other iron. He wanted to help the Zamorakians, to bring them an offering that might soften their determination to isolate. In the dragon's optimism, the gift would bring the Zamorakians into the wider Fellhollow community. 

Rasmodel had his steel. He could outfit his new order. With the misguided dragon's help, he had his knights. They would ensure the rest of Fellhollow would keep their distance.

A long-held frustration of the Zamorakian magi, the magical sect of the Children of Shadows, was that Zamorak never came to them. At no point did Zamorak ever set foot on the shores of Ashenfall. This fact is key to understanding the resentful mentality of the shadow mage: they feel a constant undercurrent of abandonment. It also explains why they believe that mystic cloth 'came from Zamorak himself'.

As mystic cloth became more available at Dowdun Reach, Lord Rasmodel made the decision to allow its trade. The trade partners would be carefully chosen - no Saradominists, obviously. It led to the first interactions between the Children of Shadows and an offshoot of the Garou. They showed no animosity to the Children; more a kind of unbridled curiousity. A friendship, of sorts, grew between Rasmodel and their merchants.

Over the span of that friendship, Rasmodel learned about their affinity to the moon, and their need to remain untethered, nomadic. He did not pry at their religion, but wondered whether this offshoot shared the same god as their counterparts.

It frustrated Rasmodel that he could not infiltrate the Moon Garou, as he did with all other civilisations: he had no one in the Reach with the physiology. So, he resolved to meet with Alric, something he deemed a last resort. 

Alric suggested something interesting. He could not change the appearance of a human to resemble the Garou: that was beyond his capabilities. But the mystic cloth: within that fabric was an opportunity. The material was known to hum, to resonate. But, like a parrot, it could also retain resonance and repeat it. With work, the mystic cloth could surveil the Moon Garou.

Perhaps Rasmodel could infiltrate them after all.

Although the name may be sinister, the robes and the people who wear them are not automatically evil. 

The most infamous dark wizards became a fairytale for the people of Brynmoor, a cautionary tale about greed and the desire for power. They sought power in the darkest of places, and it turned them into something monstrous. 

They appeared in the village in the dead of night as twisted versions of themselves—mindless creatures infused with a shadowy essence. Those who found themselves in their path had their lives ripped out of them until nought but an empty husk remained. 

It took all the visiting Wild Knights to take them down. Arrow after arrow, spell after spell, the task seemed almost insurmountable until the light of the sun made the creatures vulnerable. In their death throws, it was as if some horrible chill fell across the world as if a door somewhere had been opened into somewhere it shouldn’t.

The knights of Saradomin had many names over the years. White Knights. Temple Knights. In Ashenfall they were called Paladins. Brave, and faithful, members of noble families that took up the sword.

They were the second, third and fourth children of Brynmoor’s “nobility” – a title given to those families that were most loyal to the priesthood, and the most obedient were raised the highest.

These platelegs were exceptionally well made. Sturdier and stronger than the armour worn by Brynmoor guards, and other members of Ashenfall’s human soldiers. It was certainly stronger than the helms of the Guthixian aligned knights, or the druids they guarded.

Paladins were a righteous force, noble and true to the priest’s teachings. They brought order from chaos, and discipline to sloth. Remaking Bramblemead according to the church’s designs.

It was on a cold winter night, that Nightingale and Moragrin finally met. The assassin had given up on interrogating the villagers and worked instead to lay a trap for the highwaywoman. A caravan, loaded with gold, made its way from village to castle under the cover of night, and Nightingale arrived to claim her bounty.

It was a short, but brutal battle. Arrows whistled through the air, making music like an orchestra. Daggers glinted in the moonlight, in a dance of death that would have shredded the life of any normal person. But Nightingale and Moragrin were evenly matched, until an errant magpie brought it all to an end.

Confused by the battle, the magpie had woken from its sleep in a mad panic and dived down at the fighting pair. The distraction was just enough for Moragrin’s blade to pierce the Nightingale’s heart and as she pulled her mask from her victim, Moragrin’s world shattered as she saw the moment the life left the eyes of her younger sister.

Reinforced leather, designed to withstand powerful attacks. Layers of stiffened leather folded together to strengthen one another, turning singular sheets into a powerful defensive force. 

When an Amalgamated rose through the ranks of the guard, a tricky feat given the prejudice against them, it did not instil the populace with a sense of comfort. The villagers saw the marks of the elemental corruption on the guards and would turn and walk the other way. They would refuse to report a crime if they saw leaves instead of hair. 
Amalgamated guards would rarely be granted equipment of greater quality than reinforced leather. No matter what missions they were assigned. It was seen as a waste, considering the uncooperative nature of many villagers, making it hard for an Amalgamated guard to do their duty. 

It is, perhaps, no wonder that few Amalgamated guards chose to remain in the service for long.

Those who joining the Fellhollow military, who were not from the more noble bloodlines, would join the force known as the hoplites. This force of warrior farmers named themselves after the hoplite forces of Hallow, legendary forces spoken of in some of the Saradominist text.

When the withering began it was the hoplites that were most affected. Their unity and loyalty became a weakness, as members within their ranks fell to the affliction and then turned on the others. This shattered their synchronicity and spread paranoia and distrust among the ranks.

As their members fell, they would rise as the undead and immediately turn on their brethren, ripping the group apart from the inside.

At least in death they stand together again.

Not all druids follow the notion that one must live a life of peaceful coexistence. After all, the animal kingdom is full of conflict and conquest, predator and prey, as well as battles for dominance. Some druids feel, therefore, that to truly emulate nature one must do so fully, including tooth and claw. The problem there is that druids are, by nature, generally on the squishier side and so they needed armour that would protect their vital areas better than their normal robes.

After having her glittering armour stolen from her, Lavernius decided that she was tired of being “Miss Nice Wizard” and crafted a version of the armour that would blend worryingly well into the local scenery. She crept up upon her robbers as they spent an evening trying to prize gemstones from what remained of her helm, and she unleashed her terrible revenge.

Thousands of chinchompas, each of them suffering from a powerful confusion, were turned upon the miscreants. The resulting explosion was so bright that it turned night into day for a moment, and the flames spreads with worrying rapacity.

Later bandits soon agreed that Lavernius was to be given a very wide berth.

It was late in the development of Fellhollow that the people needed armour. They made do with old hand me down leather and bronze gear, brought with them from Bramblemead, for the most part, because what need was there for armour when you were protected by dragons.

When Kuldra arrived it was as if the air had grown heavy, pressing down on everyone. A whole village fell silent as the Godeater turned her gaze upon them. As Imaru and Xikotal rose to face her, villagers instinctively ran to don their heirloom steel armour, hoping that they wouldn‘t have to use it, but knowing in their hearts that they would.

Yet, it wasn’t Kuldra that found a use for the armour. After Xikotal’s death, Kuldra left the region, leaving Imaru to her grief. And it would be that grief and the rage that came with it that would ultimately lead to the rising dead and the sad and sorry need to fight against them.

Reinforced leather, with metal plates woven throughout. The plates rest underneath leather coverings, with metal studs to hold them in place, giving the armour its name. Favoured by scouts and hunters for the mixture of agility and defence. 

While the design of studded leather made it excellent for hunters and invaluable in battles against the garou, it fell short against those who knew its construction. Betrayal is a constant foe, and once the garou war had come to a close, the Wild Knights found themselves at the mercy of it. 

Though there were more dangerous threats, with the dragons that appeared out of nowhere, there were always some that prioritized power and persecution over common sense. Daggers emerged from the shadows and drove between the plates sewn into the leather. The poison on the tips of these blades ended the life of more than a few Wild Knights. 

The assassins called themselves the “Glass Knives” in memory of the serial killer that took the lives of so many Amalgamated. To them, the strangeness of the knights was a greater threat than the dragons, and they were happy to pay the terrible price that their actions would cost.

Trapped by a shrine that stood atop a rocky island, surrounded by the hungry dead, Sir Lateef prayed to Saradomin with all his strength. He begged for salvation, for a miracle that might free him, but silence was his only answer.

He barricaded himself atop the monument, the dead scrabbling at his defences, their unholy growling keeping him from even a moment’s sleep.

As time drew on, and hunger and thirst took him, he turned to the symbol of Saradomin in defeated rage. Where was his god? Why was he abandoned? Had he not spent his life in service of the lord of light?

Enraged, he took his sword and cleaved Saradomin’s symbol from its plinth, causing it to crash to the floor. Filled with guilt, he hid the fallen piece from his sight. His last act was one of blasphemy, but perhaps an act justly performed.

The Wild Knights were known for their ability to scout the wild areas of Ashenfall. They could walk where no one else dared to tread, and they were masters of the hunt, putting even the garou to shame.

Their archers wore sturdy leathers, using carefully laid folds to shield them against harm. They would break easily, but the archers made it part of their ritual to maintain them regularly. Each day they would wake and tend to the leather, making their armour seem brand new with each morning.

Archers worked alone, it was easier that way. They could stalk their prey, silent and slowly, sometimes taking days between kills as they followed their quarry around the lands. They would hunt monsters and people with equal focus, as dictated by the order’s commands.

To harm the wilds was to make yourself a target for the Wild Knights. To sully the green lands of Guthix, an unforgivable crime. Yet, there were always those who would risk their ire, wannabe despots, and greedy fools yearning to plunder the realm.

They all met the fate they deserved, in the end.

These blue robes are the signature clothing worn by every respectable mage. Apprentices look forward to the day they first wear these, as it shows they have graduated to full wizard status. The wizard's tower, in particular, uses these robes as a uniform, signifying their magical power and fealty to the god of order, Saradomin.

All documents have been expunged of the events that came to be known as the Day of Purple Feathers. No wizard or knight will speak of what occurred. However, villagers soon learned that squawking like a chicken would send both knight and wizard into unexplained panic.

For some reason, the egg deliveries to the Wizard's guild were cancelled around the same time.

It's hard to look at what the Zamorakian Mages have become. They were once a noble order, committed to chaos, demonology and destruction. You would fear to be in their presence, particularly that of their leader, Alric. Now, you fear them for wholly different reasons. The Reach has twisted them into something bestial. 

Before the re-routing of the Dreaming Stone's energy-flows, people weren't exposed to the energy tethers for long. But now that Dowdun Reach was a lattice of Dreaming Stone energy, that changed. The flows ran through people's rooms as they slept. It pulsed and hummed and bled.

People began to change. Dreams became rarities; nightmares the commonplace. People heard whispers in quiet moments. Through lack of both sleep and solace, the magi were frayed at the edges. They would be quick to anger.

Then the infestation came. People fell sick; a great wave moved from the magi wing outwards. The lucky were feverish or comatose, left to die on stretchers. The unlucky carried battles in their heads. Voices warred within them, and they would buckle over in pain as screaming headaches took them. 

The epidemic was so terrible, so consuming, that Lord Rasmodel's actions might be considered understandable. He was desperate, and likely ill himself. 

Lord Rasmodel shattered the Dreaming Stone with his warhammer. It smashed - its shards tossed to the winds. For one glorious moment, the voices stopped. Lord Rasmodel felt peace in that moment. But something worse was unleashed, and the horrors doubled.

A more powerful and refined wardstone, this one allows you a much greater return on investment. When activated, it draws energy from the soul more cleanly and efficiently, reducing the impact of Soulscourge on the user.

Some materials are better at resonating with souls than others. This is why a home made of better quality stones feels more comfortable than one made from pebbles. When used in wardstones, they allow for more powerful effects at no greater cost to the soul itself. Drawing out more of the ambient anima within a person, whilst minimizing the damage done.

Wardstones are handy tools for anyone working with wards. Armour, when created on an anima-rich continent like Ashenfall, is infused with threads of enchantment that harness natural magic to provide an extra layer of protection for the wearer.

This protection depletes upon use, as the energy within the ward is expended on the effect. But the wardstone can rectify this situation… for a price.

The stone around the wardstone is hollow and more brittle than it seems, and the rune essence is carefully laid within it along with a final ingredient – a drop of the creators own blood. This blood, and the rune essence formation, creates a sort of harmonious connection between the bearer of the wardstone and the stone itself.

When the stone is broken, it activates the rune essence and draws anima in from the nearest pure source – the soul of the user.

Souls are containers of pure anima. They grow over time from experience and adventure, the more the bearer of said soul enriches their life with activity, the stronger the soul. This means that the damage the wardstone creates, whilst not insignificant, is easily repaired through the simple passage of time. Especially on Ashenfall, where merely surviving to the next hour is an adventure.

Wardstones are great in a pinch, but one must be careful not to use too many, less the damage they deal to themselves becomes too great.

Wizards always push themselves too far. They compete academically with anything that moves, insisting on demonstrating their intellectual superior over everyone around them. Their intelligence is a glittering brooch that must be seen by everyone.

Naturally, this sort of mentality causes many a wizard to burn out. Academia creates a sort of stress that could be bottled and weaponised, only to be banned by conventions around the world for its cruelty. Yet, a wizard does it to themselves, pushing and pushing beyond the limits of the human condition.

Inevitably they turn to external solutions, and this particular mixture has pushed many a mage past academic burnout just long enough to get by. Somehow it makes magic flow easier, spells cast stronger than before, and the wizard is capable of feats they might never have achieved before.

They also tell themselves that it lets them get by on only two hours of sleep a night. It does not and it all catches up with them in the end. Wizard burnout can be disturbingly literal.

At first glance you might just assume this is poison, for the cadavaberry is a dangerous fruit. Yet, with careful preparation, and an appropriate amount of boiling, the berry can become a sharp, yet not fatal addition to any dessert.

Mixed with water, it becomes an empowering infusion, that puts a sprint in your step. Partially because your body is reminded at just how close it came to death in drinking it, so makes the most of the life it has left.
The delicious dwellberry infuses the water with a delightful flavour. It’s easier and far more refreshing than normal water. You’ll find this hydrates you much better than normal water.

The deer of Ashenfall are quick and nimble, able to dash across entire regions at the drop of a hat. By boiling the tainted water of Ashenfall combined with a deer’s antlers, some of this essence of swiftness is drawn into the clean water, granting anyone who drinks it a measure of that fleet-footed nature.
The distilled strength of a maple tree, pumped straight to your dietary tract. You might fell a rising fury whenever you stare at a logging axe, however.

Boiling away the tainted material, in the water, while infusing it with an animal bone, causes the mixture to draw in some of the primal resonance of the bone. Drinking the infusion temporarily allows the drinker to feel some of that animalistic ferocity.

Across dimensional rifts this drink calls out to a part of us we try and hide. That piece of us that wants a touch of whimsy, when the world turns darker than we feel comfortable with. As the approaching shadow of winter looms, its cold chill reaching into our souls, drinks like these offer a little warmth against the oncoming frost.

Still, its allure can be overwhelming. There are some who succumb too heavily to its charms, becoming thralls to the flavour. Their entire personality clasps onto this mixture with frightening zeal.

It is tasty… but at what price?
The juice of the berries infuses the water with a pleasant flavour. It’s easier and more refreshing to drink, offering greater hydrating properties than normal water.

Popular among Wild Knight scouts, this mixture focussed the senses and improved reaction times. It was taken by hunters as they explored the dangerous forests of Ashenfall, allowing them respond to threats faster than before. Those extra moments saved countless lives.

In the village of Bramblemead there was a contingent of foolish youngsters who began drinking this mixture in large quantities, due to the energetic thrill it created. It would allow them to engage in strenuous activities for far longer without getting tired.

Its popularly began to grow among older members of the population as well, though no one would be willing to explain why.

The smaller creatures can be annoyingly good at getting out of harms way. When boiling water infused with a small fang, belonging to one of these critters, this evasive essence can be pulled into the clean water, allowing the drinker to benefit from it.
Boiling the water separates the tainted elements in the liquid, allowing you to scrape it off and enjoy a more pleasant beverage.

This water will quench your thirst a little, but it contains a faint residue of a cataclysmic event that afflicted Ashenfall. As a result, there are traces of a tainted substance in the water that will damage the soul of anyone who drinks it, if they’re not a native that’s adapted to it.
The shattered remnant of the Black Knight Titan's sword.

A seemingly simple amulet carrying the symbol of Bandos. It feels strangely warm to the touch, and a faint sensation like the memory of a heartbeat thrums through your fingertips.

Zanik would be very interested in this.

A simple amulet bearing Bandos’ symbol. Every once in a while you could swear you hear strange whispers from it, like a song you can’t quite remember. It’s cold to the touch and makes the hairs, on the back of your neck, shiver constantly.

A crystal sphere containing a shimmering fog. It’s part of a magical ritual needed to craft a powerful staff, one that is infused with celestial power. The Icyene, of Hallow, once gifted these orbs to the followers of Saradomin, so that they might better serve the god of Order.

The crystal tree is a magical thing, a native to the elven world Tarddiad and brought to Gielinor when the elves migrated. Unlike most seeds it is nourished not by soil, nor sunlight, but by music. Elf song reaches into the crystal, creating a harmonious resonance that causes the crystal to thrive.

As well as growing trees, this seed can also be sung into specific shapes and forms, becoming a tool or weapon of great power.

This seed is a little different to those found in Tirannwn, infused both with the magic of Ashenfall, but also the essence of cherished memories.

General Velgar was one of Queen Kuldra’s most feared disciples. Infused with the power of the earth itself, his poison corrupted entire regions and slaughtered countless foes.

The fall of Blue Flame Keep, Bramblemead village, and the Saradominist presence on Ashenfall, was orchestrated by Velgar’s hand. His army incinerated villagers, priests and soldiers alike. His forces tore down castles and houses with equal relish.

For thousands of years, Velgar has been a thorn in everyone’s side, until one hero took him down.

Bring this to the Wise OId Man, rumours are that he can make something useful from it.

Cathan’s challenge horn design is a collection of materials hastily thrown together. A dragon’s tooth, carved with dragonkin sigils as well as metal wires used to connect them together –  like a spiderweb lattice, glistening with traces of magical power.

When placed in the dragonkin altar, the horn acts as a focus point for the magical power stored within the tower, sending an invisible beacon off into the air, in a perfect draconic pitch.

Velgar has been summoned many times before. By heroes, kings, warriors and gods, and yet still the tower stands.

Be ready, when you send out the call.

These tokens are a unique creation, crafted hundreds of years ago by high priests of Lougrim. They are passed down from monarch to monarch, a sign of their overwhelming power and strength. The power of a king has waned over the centuries, no longer sovereigns of nations, but now masters of the clan. Servants of the High Queen, Kuldra, the god-eater.

Inside each token rests a single pebble taken from Antumnos, the Garou homeworld.

These tokens are a unique creation, crafted hundreds of years ago by high priests of Lougrim. They are passed down from monarch to monarch, a sign of their overwhelming power and strength. This token has been blessed as part of an ancient Garou ritual, symbolically wiping away Gielinor's "corruption". Although it looks no different to before it was purified, it feels strangely lighter and is a little cold to the touch. 

Inside each token rests a single pebble taken from Antumnos, the Garou homeworld, and if you focus your senses you're sure you can feel the faintest pulse from within.
The remnants of an abyssal demon. Fine ashes, infused with malice. Not great for the skin, despite what Doric will tell you.
The spinal column of an abyssal demon. A monstrous creature from between the gaps in reality. It pulses with hatred, and you’re convinced it wriggles occasionally.

The magic of Ashenfall permeates the very trees of the region, imbuing their bark with mystical properties. This bark can be refined into wild anima, a magical crafting component that is popular with spellcasters of all kinds.

It is perhaps strange that bark could be referred to as hollow. It’s generally extracted in strips, or patches, but nothing that could act as a container. Indeed there would need to be an entire tree without a centre to make it hollow, and that’s exactly the case here.

When the withering began it was souls that suffered first, torn away and dragged into the underworld to be consumed by the cursed dragon. Yet, as time drew on, Imaru’s hunger did not diminish, only grow, and so the world itself began to suffer.

Things taste worse in Fellhollow. Dreams are vapid and empty, if you have them at all. A campire feels colder, even if it warms you just as well as before. Because the substance, the soul of that thing if you will, is being consumed.

Hollow bark comes from a tree that has been hollowed out from within. Its insides less than they should be, weaker and wrong. As awful as this is, it makes the bark develop in strange ways desirable by skilled mages with a poor sense of fashion.

The blood of a dragon is a strange thing. It is infused with magic in a way that most other creatures can’t match, a side effect of the alchemical processes that created their kind. Dragonkin scientists needed blood that could adapt to a wide range of chimeric additions to the body. As such dragon blood, unlike human blood, doesn’t possess any particular type and each could act as donor to the other.

Bones are not just the favoured materials of necromancers, they can be incredibly useful to a skilled survivalist as well. Designed to withstand great force, while still being as light as possible, bones make excellent components in an array of crafting recipes.

You may think an undead bone is no different from a normal bone, and in most cases that’s true. The animating forces of necromancy normally just puppet the bones, allowing them to be possessed by a spirit or controlled by some external force.

The undead of Fellhollow are different. They animated by the last remnants of their humanity, tiny bits of soul clinging on in tattered forms. They move because they need to and they shudder because they fear what happens when they stop.

As such, the bones of the Fellhollow dead have strange properties that a skilled craftsman can refine and find uses for.

Morbid, yes, but inventive.

It seems almost unfair that plants could become electrified – a secret guarded closely by eels until now. These bulbs still contain a residual trace of that lightning charge, making them much desired in herblore.

Few vegetables are as worthy of such reverence as the humble cabbage. Delicious. Easily portable and possessing of a leaf more noble than any other. This, at least, the followers of the god Brassica Prime profess.

No one is entirely sure how there is a god of cabbages. Fewer still are sure why there is a god of cabbages, yet the existence of such a being cannot be denied.

Unlike other deities, who guard the focus of their faith with great jealousy, Brassica Prime delights in nothing more than seeing people enjoying the delicious, nutritious, wonders of the humble cabbage.

Best enjoyed fried or mixed into a stew. Cabbage wine is a failed experiment that should never be spoken of again.
This sticky brown substance is rarely considered the right answer to the classic joke, however it has many more uses outside the realm of low-tier comedy.

Coal is the petrified memory of a long dead ecosystem. Plants and animals, who lived countless aeons ago, crushed down into rocks of rigid carbon and becoming a powerful fuel that changes civilisations.

When you burn the coal, imagine the worlds that came before and the lives forgotten. Let each piece become a funeral pyre for a long-lost age.

Or enjoy shiny new steel armour, your call!

There are many theories on what ectoplasm is, each as disturbing as the last. Some say that it’s emotion, condensed over years of existence. Others think that it’s some sort of infection of the soul, as only the undead seem to produce it.

In truth, ectoplasm is none of these. It’s the outer shell of a soul, the skin more or less, and acts as a sort of lubrication between the flesh and the spirit. Tiny bits of reality combine with the immaterial otherness of the underworld and congeal into a sort of spiritual mixture that ghosts and other undead find incredible useful.

Necromancers prize the stuff, finding a myriad of uses for it. Most people find it weird and decidedly “icky”.

If you prescribe to the theory of evolution, then this object wins the debate. After all, what came before the egg wasn’t technically a chicken, but something similar yet different. So, the egg would have come first, to then hatch the first chicken.

Of course, the real question here is whether chickens evolved, or whether they were created by the gods? No god claims to have created the first chicken, nor, indeed, the first egg, but they never deny it when asked.

So, which came first? The god or the egg?

Cursed essence is a magical agent often used in tanning. It is also something of a curiosity, one that a simple tanner can't readily explain. It goes further than simply binding itself to material; it latches onto it, almost parasitically. 

Demonologists have a theory that, although cursed essence drops from avernic demons of Dowdun Reach, it is actually cthonic in nature. It would explain the tendril-like tenacity.

Much more fun now that they’ve been removed from the mouth of a particularly angry critter. These teeth are surprisingly sharp, capable of piercing some armour. Scholars argue that the anima twist of Ashenfall makes the teeth more durable, whereas hunters would claim that the animals here are just more naturally belligerent than others. They prefer to use more colourful language to describe them, however.

In the Highlands of Ghornfell lay the bones of titan wyrms. These dread creatures were once the pride of the dragonkin forces. Massive beasts, with wingspans that blotted out the sun and claws that could tear down a fortress. They were destroyed in a cataclysmic battle against a powerful mage with a command over lightning, who summoned a terrible storm that still rages to this day.

The wyrms are dead now. At least, we hope they are. All that remain are their bones. The teeth are particularly prized and are a vital component in some dragonkin craftwork.
The deadly canines of a large creature. Much more fun when they’re not still in the snapping maw of the creature chasing you.

The simple feather is the greatest invention of the avian species. The aviansie race is particularly proud of their plumage and hold lengthy conversations about the best maintenance methods. For the average bird, such as magpies and similar, feathers grant them the ability to soar through the skies, defying the whims of gravity.

They are a vital part of crafting arrows, as the feathers are used for the flights that cause the arrow to fly true.

The origin of mystic fibres, thread and cloth are wreathed in mystery. There's little to no mention in any written histories, which - on its own - seems unlikely. It is not a subtle material. There should be log after log of merchants trading it, or actively avoiding trading it. 

It leads me to believe that the mystic material is an elaborate troll. It's a plant - and I don't mean that in the floral definition. I think someone has introduced it as a joke, and I have a list of potential culprits. Brassica Prime: you are number one on that list.

Flax was a popular plant during the time of Guthix. Found all over the continent, it could be easily harvested and refined into various materials. Villagers would enjoy the seeds as tasty snacks, but they mostly prized the flax for the threads that could be woven from the stems of the plant.
Very popular with newbie argonauts.
Bundles of animal fur, scratchy and thick. While not an ideal trait on pets, this sort of coarse fur has a multitude of uses in the wild.
So fluffy.

The corpse flower is a strange creature. Strange, almost flesh-like sacks, filled with a noxious gas that explodes upon contact with the air. The resulting mass of fibres looks disturbingly like thin strips of a cadaver’s skin, earning it the name “corpse cotton” by the more macabre Emberwood villagers.

In truth it’s little more than thick plant fibres that can be woven together into a smooth, durable and, frankly, fashionable fabric.

The more green the colour of jade, the higher the concentration of iron in its makeup. Popular among many cultures for both its beauty and it’s use as a crafting material.

In the eastern lands of Wushanko, jade is highly prized. Some islands have elaborate rituals about its usage. There are rumours of rare jades with magical properties, each a different colour of the rainbow.
A very shiny stone. Prized by magpies around the world, who are drawn to it’s magnificent luster.

This glittering gemstone really catches the light, sparkling like a tiny little star that’s dying. Count Valingard, one of Brynmoor’s nobility in the third age, was very fond of these stones and was said to have an entire chandelier made from them. Sadly, dragon fire appears to be particularly good at melting them, so the chandelier is no longer with us.

Your eyes are like sapphires, sparkling in the night
Every syllable you utter creates pure delight.
A day in your company is infinitely bright
Will you be my valentine tonight?

A poem by a lovestruck Bramblemead youth. It was not successful.

Aside from their use in terrible teenage poetry, sapphires are a vital component in jewellry crafting. They can be found hidden among the metal ores around Ghornfell, or hidden in the various chests left behind by the old kingdom.

This stone is stubbornly resistant to magic. Whereas other stones absorb the nascent anima, granite refuses. It exists on its own terms, not the whims of magic. As such it is highly prized in construction projects, as it resists most magic and what enchantments do affect it, last much longer.

Legend speaks of a weapon forged from this stone. A great hammer, said to be able to crush almost any armour.

The handle for a powerful weapon. Wrapped in sturdy leather and imbued with ritual magic. A dwarf capable of crafting such a piece is acknowledged to have completed one of the trials required to be a master smith.
A chunk of granite imbued with powerful magic. On its own, this hunk of stone isn’t useful, but when combined with other components it becomes part of a much stronger weapon.
These wrappings are made from the leather of a powerful and dangerous beast. The ambient anima of Ashenfall infuses it with a sturdiness that most leather wrapping can’t equal.
A bitter tasting herb that makes for a poor tea, yet is often used as a morning pick-me-up by people with no tastebuds. It’s useful in crafting certain potions.

The heart is a pump, nothing more, designed to push precious blood around the circulatory system, so that our weak and feeble frames can function. Yet, metaphysically, it is the most important organ, for it houses our emotional centre, the very essence of what makes us human.

A withered heart isn’t just a heart that has languished. Nor is it just a heart that is drained of blood. No, a withered heart has had all the humanity leeched from it, emptied of emotion, or connection, of self.

This process is imperfect, of course, and tiny fragments of soul-matter remains, that can be refined and extracted from the heart. Withdrawing the last remnants of what made the owner “them”.

Animal hides have a variety of uses and have made hunters very popular throughout history. In Bramblemead there was an annual competition to see who could find the most interesting hide. While anima twisted animals were plentiful, many hunters turned to cheating, leading to a rise in the dye trade.

Do not pity the dragonwolf. Oh, you don’t? That’s fine then. Yes, horrible little blighters aren’t they? Their teeth are too jagged and they’re far too aggressive for my tastes. Still, apparently deadly critters don’t just want to roll over and die so you can harvest their skin, who’d have thought?

The dragonwolf is an experiment that didn’t quite go right. It wasn’t wrong, exactly, it was certainly a step in the direction their creator wanted, but like most Dragonkin experiments, they was never “good enough”. Too warm-blooded. Too fluffy. Too weak.

Fortunately, the crafters in Emberwood were not quite so picky. They found that hide to be incredibly durable and hardy, whilst remaining surprisingly flexible. They would craft wonderous outfits from the tanned leather, trying to emulate their winged overseers.

Blacksmiths hate this one simple trick. They spend their lives mastering interlocking chains and plates, only for dragons to be born with them. A third of the blacksmith's punters are now gone, hunting for dragons rather than dropping some coin at their forges. It's enough to make a blacksmith sob into their anvil. 

Blue dragons hate that one simple trick too, of course. Their venn diagram overlaps with the blacksmith's: they'd rather people got their armours from somewhere other than dragons.

Dragons are never fond of surrendering their natural armour, a natural feature they’ve used to great advantage.

In the earliest days of the Dragonkin experiments, the scales of a dragon were something to be proud of. They would turn away blade and arrow with equal skill, and even magic struggled to pierce their natural defences.

Still, this wasn’t good enough for their makers. Lacrussa, herself, saw the hide of dragons as a promising start, but little more, and she would strive to forge newer and more resilient scales on her creations. The green dragons were, in her opinion, some of the weakest of the dragons, and she only kept them around because her sibling enjoyed the glittering green reflections of the sunlight on their scales.

A collection of pelt scraps collected from smaller creatures. Some druids would insist on crafting cloaks made only from smaller animals that died of natural causes. This meant that each cloak often took months, or sometimes even years, to craft. Naturally this wasn’t particularly helpful on the cusp of winter.

Fragments of thick and sturdy hides, these scraps are of little use on their own, but combined together they can become a resilient leather with a wide range of uses.

The Children of Shadows would scavenge these scraps across Fellhollow, using the pieces to craft profane totems to their sinister god. Unaware that he would be offended at such a low-effort offering.

A tougher, more resilient hide, obtained after slaying a deadly dire wolf. It is said that if you wear the hide of a dire wolf, during the light of a full moon, then you’ll rapidly reduce the size of your circle of friends.

Nature’s attempt to better the blacksmith’s craft, these powerful horns can do a lot of damage to their target when correctly applied. Fortunately they have a number of uses outside of skewering one’s organs, making them a useful resource prized by all skilled hunters and survivalists.

The weapons of the animal kingdom can make for useful crafting components. Unfortunately, you generally have to battle against said weapons in order to use them. Many a hunter has underestimated how damaging an animal horn can be.

It is not a mistake they repeat.

A little like a conch, you can put this infernal shard to your ear. But instead of hearing the sea, you might hear the mumblings of an ancient mind. It's no less soothing, though. If you ever have trouble sleeping, we recommend the calming white-noise of a fractured malevolent soul.

It is unsurprising that irit grows in Fellhollow, considering it has such a close association with the dead. Whereas normal irit grows around graveyards and near the temples of death deities, the irit in Fellhollow grows everywhere.

Yet, unlike its leafier version, the irit here is twisted and sharp. It is formed of thorny roots that writhe out of the ground and must be cut down to access the herb beneath. After all, the dead in Fellhollow refuse to stay buried without a fight, why wouldn’t the irit react the same?

This herb has travelled far to deliver its malicious benefits to the people of Ashenfall. Its name, in Dwarvish, means “warm death” since it causes a terrible burning fever if the sap is consumed.

Crushed down, so that it’s sap and oils can be easily accessed, this herb is a requirement in some of the deadliest and most dangerous poisonous mixtures.

Turn your limestone this way and that and you might come across a fossil. Lesser blue dragons have been in the region for some time, and there were experimentations that led to them. Being experimentations, they weren't cognitively blessed, so it's entirely possible to find the talon of a dragon that got trapped in a rockfall.

You could also find the odd chicken wing from the Second Age. The Children of Shadows loved a shake and bake.
A sweet smelling herb that is used as an ingredient in many poison remedies.
Thick and tough to chew, this meat makes for a filling meal, but an exercise to bite through.

The white meat of a bird is surprisingly versatile. While delicious on its own, it can be combined with various dishes, seasoned or marinated to create surprisingly varied flavours. The meat is often softer to consume, making it an easier meal for the on-the-go hunter.
This dense meat is a sturdy meal when prepared properly. It comes from larger animals who are far less willing to give it up than their smaller kin, making it a well deserved delicacy.

Tender meat, straight from the hunt. Traditionally you would hang the meat to dry out a little before consumption, marinating it in wines, spices and preservatives. However, you can lob it in a fire if you want a more immediate meal. We don’t judge.

Smaller creatures have much less meat on their bones, making them a less fulfilling meal. Hunters would subsist on these while stalking larger prey over several days. The Wild Knight Ilom was particularly fond of drying out these little morsels, treating them as a meaty little snack for boring patrols.

Despite the appearance of their former owners, and the deeply unpleasant smell emanating from it, undead meat is not rotten. It, like the nature of its former owners, is preserved in a state between life and death, as if it’s on the cusp of souring, but never quite getting there.

This unique nature gives it some very interesting properties, but sadly taste isn’t one of them.
While many mushrooms are poisonous, the Bittercap Mushroom turns against the defences of its kin and is an ingredient in several poison remedies.

Once the home to a chirping little critter, this nest now best serves as a means of gathering materials. Don’t feel bad about destroying a little bird’s home, they were saying horrible things about you anyway. I mean really horrible, like so, so bad.
Often used in metaphors about layers. Also soups. The soups are more fun.

Sometimes colours just want to manifest themselves. There is the sweetness of orange. The cowardice of yellow, and the peaceful serenity of a summery green. Blue, tired with only being linked to sorrow and sadness, sought a physical existence. One that could be shaped into new and wonderous things, and so blurite was born.

A metal that is so very, very, blue.

It’s in the name. After all, it’s blue, right?
Shiny and metallic, combine with tin to make bronze bars.

Few things have led to quite so much wanton destruction as gold. Wars have been fought over it. Brother has slain brother just for a handful of the glimmering nuggets. Yet, without the trappings of commerce, it is but a metal like any other. Pretty though, good in jewellry.

Iron is sturdy, durable and malleable. Blacksmiths prize it highly as a material for crafting superior arms and armour from. It is rumoured that certain fae creatures are repelled by the ore, perhaps due to it’s rigidity and refusal to change to the whims of magical beings.

Mithril means much to the dwarves. It was first discovered by a dwarf called Tolkun in the First Age, and its seams have been uncovered in much of Gielinor. The dwarves call it 'The Dwarven Welcome' thanks to its shine. Find the ore when mining and it lights up to greet you: a dwarven welcome. 

A 'Dwarven Welcome' is also what dwarves call a headbutt while wearing a mithril helmet.

Silver is an excellent conductor of magic. It’s why it shows up in so many legends and myths about supernatural foes. As a precious metal, it is highly prized in the crafting of jewellry and strongly disliked by lycanthropes everywhere.
Shiny and metallic, combine with copper to make bronze bars.
Amid the sulphurous pile are some items. Don't be scared: reach a hand in and claim what's yours.
The magnificent bounty of a demonic kill. Want to know what an infernal creature carries around with them on their day-to-day? Look no further!

These leathery packs are carried around by garou warriors. They store rations, herbs and the spoils of battle inside. Crafted by elderly garou in the villages, or parents sending their child off to battle.

This foul smelling container is made from animal hide and filled with whatever trinkets its owner collected. Occasionally a goblin will keep a pet beetle, worm or spider in these, so be careful when opening.
A foul-smelling sack, with threads frayed by the cruelty of time. Contains whatever strange possession were being hoarded after death.
The knapsack of a mage within Dowdun Reach. It contains all the materials that the mage would have needed for a day's work. Now they are yours.
A bag of personal effects carried by the warrior class of Dowdun Reach. Rummage around inside: you've earned it.
A large sack covered in mould and mildew. Once belonged to the terrifying zogres that wander Fellhollow.
A decayed and blighted sack. Contains the meagre belongings of the restless dead.

Dropped by poisonous enemies.

Nature is full of malicious little critters who carry toxins. Snakes, spiders, politicians, each of them has ways to make other creatures suffer.

This ichor is prized by poisoners and alchemists, as it can be used to create deadly concoctions that will give someone, or something, a really bad day.

Baked, fried, mashed, there are few vegetables as versatile as the potato. Buried beneath the soil, these tubers absorb the nutrients around them, ready to be ripped from the soil and consumed.

Some mages noticed that you could pass certain magical currents through them, and one even attempted to enchant one and fashion it into blades. Suffice to say their plan was not successful, but it did create the very first chip, so was largely considered to be a good thing.

They seem strange without faces, don’t they?

Heralds of the spookiest of seasons, the humble pumpkin exists in two worlds, the world of the living and the world of the dead. When carved it acts as a ward against dangerous spirits and when mixed with spices and added to warm drinks, it acts as a lure for ex-girlfriends, distracting them as it gives you time to escape.
Gather 10 spectral chinchompa skulls and bring them to Vannaka in Fellhollow.

A magical stone with the ability to absorb the ambient anima around it. On Ashenfall, the rocks hover in the air as if resting on some invisible structure, held aloft by invisible currents of magical energy.

With the right equipment and skill, rune essence can be infused with certain magical resonances, taking on the elemental power of the air, or the flame.

This discovery is one of Guthix’s greatest triumphs and perhaps his longest lasting miracle.
Scraps that can be searched through. Take a look inside.
A rock with a grainy texture. I really don’t know what you’re expecting here, there’s no deep lore about sandstone.

Bloodwood trees are a genuinely horrible creation. Their very existence a wound in the decency of the world, because this tree grows only in places steeped in violence and death.

The biggest bloodwood trees tower over battlefields. Sinewy spiderlike branches cast shade over the homes of serial killers, and you should never trust anyone that can grow one in their garden.

Leech-like roots soak up the suffering, sorrow and cruelty that has seeped into the soil, and then converts that misery into a sticky red sap of raw emotion. This sap can be used to amplify feelings, to convey anger, fear or hatred, across a much greater range.

The dragonkin Lacrussa was very fond of this medium, and used it’s power to shackle dragons to her will.

It's like the maple tree wants to be cut down. Not only does it produce hardy, versatile wood for tools and weaponry, it bleeds sap that is only a single step away from being maple syrup. If you want proof of the gods' existence in RuneScape, you need only look at maple trees. 

The maple tree gets its revenge in subtle ways. Its sap is inordinately sticky and too much syrup will give you a paunch. They're small victories, but the maple tree takes them.

There is a curious trend on Gielinor, whereby the most fragile fighters - archers and magic users - are the easiest to spot. From blue dragonhide to mystic robes, the fashion is to wear garish colours as if the rules of the animal kingdom apply. But no, archers and magic users aren't poisonous, venomous or hoping to be mistaken for a bee.

We wonder why that lack of subtlety might be. In the case of blue dragons and their scales, perhaps it's a cruel joke played by the dragonkin. They have seen that humans keep killing their creations, wearing their hides as trophies. They've got their revenge by way of a generous use of the rainbow.

Fun fact: black metal is actually steel that has gone through a form of vulcanisation. Demonic or draconic sulphur are used in the process, to slightly different effect. Demonic vulcanisation gives the metal a matte finish, while draconic is more chromatic. 

What you have here is some scraps of black metal. Could be demonic, could be draconic. They're likely off-cuts from the smithing or vulcanisation process, or it's entirely possible that they were bashed off a Black Knight's armour when you boffed them. Regardless, waste-not, want-not. Now you have the material required for some new gear.

Dropped by Bleed inflicting enemies.

Small collections of sharp little objects. Stone, metal, bone, each of these combines to create a little bit of hate in the palm of your hand. The brutal points of these bits of shrapnel can tear the skin and rend the flesh, making them perfect for crafting weapons to make things bleed.

Children are often disappointed that this plant does not resemble an actual dragon. There are no snapping motions in these colourful leaves, and a depressing lack of firebreathing lizards in the stems.
A piece of a soul, half devoured by Imaru. Death seeks these to try and restore what he can of the lost, so that they might move on to the Afterlife.

Sticks and stones may break your bones, which makes them greatly desired in warfare. Stone can be found everywhere. In the grass, behind bushes, hiding in an uncomfortable part of your shoe just waiting for the right time to strike.
A weed found in desert regions – haha just kidding, swamps. It’s a weed found in swamps. I don’t know what else to tell you.

Once this tar was some living creature, perhaps and ancient dragon or other colossal beast. Time and pressure refined it down into this sticky substance that can be used for a variety of crafting purposes.

One of the most popular plants with witches everywhere, as much as for the name as its use in herblore. One witch, a particularly odd duck called Henrietta, once tried to combine the herb with the amphibian of its namesake. The resulting monstrosity killed five people, before it could finally be put down.
Squishy red little things, these fruits are a staple in sauces, salads or hurled at people in the stocks when you want to laugh at their misfortune.

A heart of alchemically modified rune essence. Layers of complex enchantments, and anima veins run through this core. It allows it to absorb ambient anima from its surroundings, which in term power the various functions of the vault guardian servitors.

This marvel of dragonkin science had revolutionary potential. The ability to create power sources that thrived off of the ambient anima of the region.
