Ashenfall, with it’s surfeit of magical power, was the perfect place for such a technology and the dragonkin would apply it to all of their arcane machinery.

A shard of rune essence, refined and manipulated by dragonkin alchemy. Tiny lines, like veins, run through the shard in very specific patterns, allowing the precise control of anima flow through the construction. When used correctly, these shards can create magical circuits, allowing the vault guardians to move, sense and attack with impunity.

It was the dragonkin Lacrussa that discovered this technology and it paved the way for powerful servitors to be constructed. Ultimately she would consider the technology a failure, albeit a useful one.

Demons do not leave bones behind on death. That would require a skeleton, a prospect that would make a demon queasy. Instead, demons are magical entities, composed of essence and energies. On death, they disperse, leaving behind an ashy composite.

Don't be mistaken in thinking that it's similar to the ash left after a fire. Dab your tongue in vile ash and you'll soon understand where the 'vile' comes from.

Drawn from pools of wither-rot water, lakes and streams poisoned by the waters of the river Noumenon – the river of the dead.

These waters have become corrupted by Imaru’s hunger and each drop longs to devour one’s soul.

Sometimes it’s not enough to drink water, you desire the urge to chomp on it instead. These melons are delicious and refreshing in ways that few other fruits seem able to match.

Smashing one with a hammer is not recommended, but still manages to be weirdly satisfying.

Even the plant world has its outcasts. Weeds are unloved, unwanted and yet they always find a way to turn up in the most unexpected of places. Just like my cousin Terry.

Still, at least weeds have some uses... unlike Terry.
These humble grains are the staple of many a burgeoning society. Breads, cakes, pastries and all manner of delicious foodstuffs can be forged from these marvellous stalks.

What ash logs lack in durability, they make up for in availability. Found all over Brynmoor, ash trees became a vital resource to the humans that resided there. Their druids would enchant the products of the trees, so that they did not rot, nor crumble against time’s assault. This would allow the trees to regrow before it would be necessary to cut them down again, creating a sustainable system.

There is something about the swamps of Ashenfall that encourage strange trees to grow. The result is blightwood, durable, powerful and somehow capable of channeling more potent magic than other trees.

The wild knights joked that blightwood houses the souls of the victims of the swamp, and that they are imbued with their regrets and hatred.

It’s probably not true. Probably.

Maple is a versatile tree. It's a formidable material, making some of the finest fishing rods, staves and bows. Its sap is also a glorious thing, capable of being made into nature's nectar: maple syrup.

Its leaf is also smooth and large enough that it can be folded over numerous times, which makes it useful if you're caught short in the forest. Which, we should add, is not, and will not be, a consideration for you. It is extremely unlikely that a 'being caught short', hunger-like bar will be added for you to maintain. Realism can be pushed too far.

Stronger than ash, the oak tree strands proud against its lesser cousin. Oak logs are more durable, sturdier and more desirable for more advanced construction. Though the trees are harder to find in Brynmoor, it is said that Ghornfell offers more of them, if one is willing to face the dangers of exploring it.
A highly efficient fuel. Required for smelting more advanced metals.

Simple wheat, water, yeast and salt, combined together and incinerated within an oven. A staple across many cultures and species across Gielinor and beyond. The Icyene of Hallow enjoy a flatbread that they can wrap food in, for a meal on the wind. The aviansie of Abbinah, when the limited wheat is available, prefer a thick and sturdy bread, so that each bite lasts longer. Elves enjoy a sourdough, priding themselves on how long they can keep the starter alive.

For the people of Ashenfall, much like their cousins in the mainland, a simple loaf is more than enough.

Brassica Prime – the god of cabbages – gifted the world with this delicious vegetable, to lead the faithful on the noble path of nutrition. In his infinite, leafy, wisdom, he taught the early settlers about how one might fry the leaves, so that they would be even more delicious.

Now these fried cabbages offer those who consume them, the strength and stamina needed to brave the hardships of Ashenfall.

Don’t eat them. No, seriously, don’t. At least not raw. Cadavaberries are poisonous and deadly, often used in sinister ceremonies of ritual sacrifice. The cadavaberries on Ashenfall are equally toxic, but can be coaxed into good behaviour with the judicious addition of fire.

The toxins can be burned away, providing a surprisingly pleasant tasting berry beneath. But, one must be careful, for preparing them wrong is the last mistake someone is likely to make.
The heat makes them edible, but only just. A once popular snack among the people of Fellhollow, who seemed overly cavalier about the consumption of deadly poisons.

Dwellberries on Ashenfall are heartier than their mainland counterparts and make for excellent ingredients. The Wild Knights were particularly fond of this combination, though they referred to them as “Dwellberry splats” because Olis, their inventor, wasn’t a particularly good chef.
Although a crude facsimile of gnomish cooking, the redberry crunchy is a pleasant snack that will get you through the day.

Imagine it. You gather around the table, the warm smell of a freshly cooked roast wafts across the room. Your mother places a bowl of roast potatoes, so divinely crisp that you wonder if they’re even real. Your sibling places a tray of freshly cooked vegetables, a kaleidoscope of colourful foods waiting to be consumed. Your father, doggedly sticking to traditional gender roles as if the past is an anchor weighing him down, slices into the roast serving each of you juicy cuts of meat that make your mouth water.

And what are you doing in all of this? Hrmm? Nothing, that’s what! Pull your life together Stanley!

If your name is not Stanley, you can ignore that above message.

Here on Ashenfall you do not have those smiling faces to reflect upon, only the memories elucidated by the roast meat before you. As you tuck in, you’ll recall those moments of happier times, before you got stranded here with nothing.
Pretty blue berries, go well in a cheesecake.
Delicious berries. They make a great snack. Very popular among garou druids as a seasoning with their latest quarry.

Slimy, greasy and everything you need. There are few things as surprisingly satisfying as a fried egg sandwich. If only you had some bread!

Contrary to popular humour the aviansie have no issue with people eating eggs around them. Indeed many of them have raptor like tastes and will happily consume the eggs of livestock birds. Boiled almhas (a domesticated bat-like creature) eggs were popular snacks on Abbinah, yet those eggs could not be fried as they would combust dramatically when exposed to a naked flame.

Fortunately chicken eggs are far less flammable.
These fillets go surprisingly well with most side dishes. The texture is pleasant, the flavour is subtle and not too overpowering. They go particularly well with garlic.

The grease. The sizzling scents. The feeling of your arteries withering just from a single glance at the dish. Is there anything in life as close to the divine as a nice, cheeky fry up?

Popular among those who have consumed a few too many gnomish cocktails the night before, a cheeky fry up is hailed as a great cure for the pounding headache that follows such indulgences.
Crisp and delicious vegetables, neatly fried mushrooms, there are few breakfasts quite as delicious as a fry up, even for those who abjure bacon.

A thick and juicy steak, combined with a rich and sticky dwellberry sauce. Each mouthful is like a taste explosion, and it can be difficult to hold yourself back. Orik the Bear was a Wild Knight who excelled in this dish, and other Wild Knights would seek him out at the gatherings for his culinary expertise.

Dwellberries boiled down into a sticky sauce and liberally trailed across the meat. At first you might expect the combination wouldn’t work, but as you take that first mouthful, you find your tastebuds writhe in delight for this delicious combination.
A thick, delicious and juicy flank steak, glazed with subtle redberry tones. Delicious and nutritious.
Flavourful roasted meat. A comforting meal to enjoy in the wilds of Ashenfall.

The Drunken Dwarf delights in the humble kebab, offering it to any that might grab his attention. The delicious combination of spices and subtle flavourings, makes it a marvellous meal. For those afflicted, or self-inflicted, with particular poisonings, the kebab is a time-honoured remedy.
A delicious pastry crust seals the flavours inside. Warm and filling, each bite designed to fill you with comfort. The flavour is earthy, and rich, letting you shrug off almost anything.

A delicious pizza, covered in juicy tender pieces of meat, and seasoned with a delicious sauce. You might ask yourself where the cheese comes from, but it’s best not to think too hard about it. Ashenfall has many secrets and those who survive here must do many strange things. No one judges you for this “cheese”, it’s fine.

Just enjoy the delicious flavours and try not to have an existential crisis.
Satisfying, if a little chewy.
For the carnivore in you, a plate of meat mixed with meat.

If you thought the mixed grill was a monumental mountain of meat, then the mixed platter makes it seem like a molehill. Thick and juicy steak, mixed with other delicious meat dishes.

Garou would regularly make dishes such as this. Mountains of meat, crafted from their latest hunts. They would flavour the dish with local herbs, and perhaps threaten it with a vegetable once in a while.

A great meal for a real fun guy… sorry.

Popular with terrible pun writers and villagers alike, roasted mushrooms were a staple diet across much Ashenfall. Wild Knights would forage for the best mushrooms to enjoy on their long hunts.

For those braving the poison filled Bloodblight Swamps, the fortifying effect of mushrooms made them even more popular.

Get a nose peg for this one. The aroma that wafts from this culinary creation is almost unholy in its sensation. It reaches down from your nostrils into your throat, then it winds its way into your mouth and twists your tastebuds in ways that must surely be illegal.

Still, if you can fight through the stench, then this eggy abomination is surprisingly tasty and helps fortify your body against toxic elements.

The “if” is doing a lot of work in that statement though.
Although the preparation leaves you weeping, and the smell makes it worse, fried onions are a delicious meal to get you through a difficult day.

Try not to think too hard about the nature of transmutation as you consume this fruit. It's sweet, juicy and the pseudo-necromantic origins only make it a little disturbing. Oh, sure, it tastes nice and it goes surprisingly well in a crumble, but it's a little disturbing when you get that unexpected crunch. 

Best not to think about it. Ignorance is bliss.

Grannies everywhere are masters of this dish. Delicious fruits, a touch of sugar, some delightful pastry and that final ingredient – a dark blessing to ancient gods.

Or love, that works too.

With the pact complete, these pies allow you those fond memories, of comfort, hope and family. Something much needed out in the wilds of Ashenfall.

The smell from this pie is strange. Undertones of gamey meats, the tantalising touch of subtle herbs and spices. The pastry is crip, inviting…and yet, beneath the crust, hidden behind the pleasantries, is the scent of something otherworldly.

Death has touched this pie.
The simplicity. The elegance. Few things in life are quite as dependable as a good old baked potato. Crispy exterior, fluffy interior, and it goes well with just about anything.

Whilst some prefer to hollow out a pumpkin, carving features into it that should scare away the creatures of the night, more practical people eat them.

A roasted pumpkin is a hearty affair, full of texture and a mellow taste that goes especially well in desserts.

These simple berries might be the gap between life and death, if eaten at the right time. A favourite treat for the villagers of Bramblemead, who would mix the berries into various desserts to enjoy. The red berry pie was particularly popular with visiting Wild Knights.
Easier, than standard redberries, to eat on the go, tastier too.
Delicious roasted meat, with a redberry glaze. The fruit flavour combines with the meat in a sublime manner that makes it hard to eat this slowly.

First discovered by a Lumbridge chef after a kitchen fire burned his mentor to ashes. Beneath the master chef’s hat was a deliciously roasted rat. Sure, it may not be the greatest delicacy of all time, but it’ll keep hunger at bay.

Not only is the roast pumpkin sandwich 'glazed' because it is doused in delicious maple syrup; it is also 'glazed' in the modern parlance, since it is 'showered with excessive, unwarranted or exaggerated praise'. Which makes it double-glazed. Like a window.

Hold up, hold up: are you sure you want to do this? You are about to cross the meat streams. Flank steak and rat roast: it's never been done before. Who knows how the two meat types might react between slices of bread.
The 'twist' in the meat twist is a delightful redberry jus. The 'twist' in the Anima Twist is not, unfortunately, a delightful redberry jus.

Steak, if you ask me, is infinitely diminished by putting it between two slices of bread. Steak should stand proud on its own, garnished with salt and barely introduced to a flame.

As a journal-writer I can't stop you. My powers are limited to shaming you from the dark recesses of your interfaces.

Brassica Prime approves of this sandwich.

Although, the mushrooms are a little much: they could probably have been thinned out. Maybe removed? Yes, remove them. The bread, too. Is it needed? Would you perhaps consider removing the envelope of bread that houses the sweet cabbage missive? Your dribbly jowls will thank us.

Sausages are proof that ignorance is bliss. When you bite into the juicy banger, you are best served not to think about its contents. Nor about the origins of the skin that contains the meat so well.

Although simple, and lacking in much needed herbs and spices, these sausages manage to curb your hunger well enough to help you avoid thinking too much about what they’re made from.

Ignore the squeak… and the whiskers.

It is said that Guthix created the first fortifying soup, as a gift for his chief druids. While the first few attempts created terrible food poisoning -- Guthix was a god, not a chef -- the perfected recipe soon became a favourite once he mixed in mushrooms. This was because the mushrooms absorbed much of the toxins, making it much more palatable.

The Wild Knights would prepare this soup before heading into the poisonous Bloodblight swamp, or when facing venomous monsters. The mushrooms would absorb some of the toxins they encountered, and the soup would be filling enough to last them through the long journeys.

As the Bramblemead villagers discovered heartier vegetables, their recipes improved just as much. The simple soup now felt richer, tastier and more fulfilling. A nice, hearty, meal to get them through the tough jobs on the farm.

A taste of autumn reduced to slurry and warmed over the fire. In Fellhollow, pumpkin soup was a popular dish when the temperature started to drop. Locals would gather together and share enormous cauldrons full of soup, as a way to build bonds with their community. It is said that once the villagers created an enormous cauldron, so that Imaru and Xikotal could join in the tradition, though for the great dragons it was barely an appetiser.
A good soup won’t just fill your stomach, it’ll lift your spirits. Warm and comforting, the taste of home. Popular across all of Gielinor by anyone with a pan and some vegetables.

Some meals require silence. As you chew each mouthful, you reminisce on times past. On loved ones lost. On friends remembered. On moments that bring a smile to your face – simple and uncomplicated, without the weight of the world’s burdens crushing down on you. This is one of those meals.

Sizzling on the grill, there is little that has united middle-aged villagers than a steak seared to perfection. Brynmoor villagers would gather around grills to discuss hunting, village politics and anything else that came to mind, all as an excuse to silently judge the way the current chef prepared this simple meal.
Thick and juicy, the flank steak is more filling than you might first expect. The dense meat means each mouthful makes you fuller for longer.

Despite the appearance of their former owners, and the deeply unpleasant smell emanating from it, undead meat is not rotten. It, like the nature of its former owners, is preserved in a state between life and death, as if it’s on the cusp of souring, but never quite getting there.

This unique nature gives it some very interesting properties, but sadly taste isn’t one of them.

Tomatoes go well with just about everything. They enhance the flavours of anything they’re paired with and a nice beef stew is no exception. The combined flavours are comforting as well as filling, and almost make you forget that you’re a long way from home, on a continent filled with murderous dragons.

A thick and tasty stew, rich in flavour and nutrients. Bramblemead’s head chef, Leander, use to say that eating this stew is like sitting around a warm fire with friends, the aroma of the rich gravy filling your nostrils makes you feel like you’re home. Villagers would gather when he made a big cauldron of the stew, each of them ready for an evening with friends.
A classic meal across multiple realities. The aviansie enjoy a version of this using insects called Tobi’it. Goblins make their meat stews out of anything they can find… results vary.

We are yet to find something that maple syrup does not elevate. Pancakes and bacon are the obvious ones, but try it out on things that are more left-field. Rat meat? Elevated. Terrorbird meat? Elevated. Undead eel? Eelevated. 

So, thank you, humble maple tree. You are a true wonder of the culinary and apothecary world. It won't stop us woodcutting you, but know that each cut comes with a maple syrup-sized dollop of guilt.

Ah, the sweet gooey goodness of a maple toastie. On its own, the maple syrup might have been too much, but the dried redberry? It ties it together with a little red bow.

There's the added bonus that it's bursting with natural sugars. Now you can use those energies to go mine and woodcut some natural resources.
Somehow still juicy, even after a judicious application of fire.

Popular among gnomes, this sticky ball of fruits and vegetables is a combination of savoury and sweet that almost feels like it shouldn’t work. Yet it does.

There is a crispness to the outer shell, savoury and salty, which gives way to a delicious fruity sweetness beneath.

Gnomish ambassadors would create large numbers of these sweet treats, piled high on silver platters, to delight visiting dignitaries at lavish receptions. Diplomats would often joke about being spoiled by such treats. Which is ironic really, as these spoil very quickly, so enjoy them now while you can.

Magic lives in the dirt, the ore and the rocks. It crystallises inside gemstones, matrices of magical energy just waiting to be unlocked. In Brynmoor the priests of Saradomin kept the secrets of magical amulets within their purview. They would craft jewellery that would enhance the fighting instinct of their soldiers, and hand then out like candy.

As the faith in Saradomin rose, the faith in Guthix waned. Partially because of the more active and present proselytising of the Saraodminist priests, who where more vocal than the quiet druids, but also because the paladins and bronze knights seemed significantly stronger than the Guthix-aligned guard. These amulets being their secret weapon.

It is perhaps not surprising that wizard’s made use of magical jewellery as early as possible. Indeed the first few pieces worn on Ashenfall were little more than decoration, and gaudy wizards would attempt to outdo one another with shinier and shinier pieces.

As part of their ever increasing “prank war” wizards began to create jewellery that was enchanted with effects designed to frustrate, or otherwise hinder, the wearer, believing that enchantments in jewellery were little more than silly trinkets. One mage created what she assumed was an auditory illusion, within an amulet, the intention being that when the wearer would try and cast a spell, the jewellery would play an annoying earworm tune, distracting the poor caster.

This did not happen. Something about the threads of magic, the structure of the crystal and the concealing spell that was intended to hide the prank, created something unique. Instead of making it harder to cast spell, the wearers found it easier. Understanding came faster, spells and incantations clearer than before.

What was intended to distract instead focused the wearer, allowing them to concentrate in ways they had never been able to before.

The Wild Knights used many tools and tricks in their hunts. Looking for any edge they might have against the untamed wilds of Ashenfall. One jeweller, a water-Amalgamated by the name of Lochlan, created an amulet imbued with the communal spirit of the hunters. He would leave the gemstone, that would serve as its base, in the gathering hall and soon it became a tradition for each hunter to touch the gemstone if their hunt was successful.

Over the years this gemstone took on their pride, prowess and skill and that became a magical enchantment that lay within the heart of the stone. Crafting an amulet from the crystal, Lochlan found a way of sharing that enchantment with everyone who wore it.

Now, millennia later, the echoes of that enchantment linger within the crystals, just waiting for someone to remember them.

The name is a bit of a misnomer, since this amulet has very little to do with the river Salve. Instead its name refers to the crystal that traditionally sits at the heart. This crystal was first brought to the region by Saradominist priests, some time in the third age. They had harvested it from a cave near the river Salve and found ways to cultivate on Ashenfall’s soil, by utilising the natural magic infusing everything.

Sanctified by faith and magic, this amulet was deadly to the undead, and the dragonkin Lacrussa was fascinated with the properties. She stole the recipe for its creation and hid it within her vault, long before the withering curse spread over Fellhollow. Instead she had delved too deeply in her own experiments and discovered something buried within the earth that would twist and reanimate the dead.

This amulet allowed her to hold the forces back and keep them from overrunning her lab. That was, until, she met her end at Kuldra’s fangs.

The Wild Knights knew the dangers of missing their target. If an arrow did not slay its target quickly, then too soon would the quarry either escape, or, worse, gather friends. Wolf packs could easily tear a small group of Knights to pieces if not dealt with fast.

The Amulet of Accuracy made sure that hunts went smoothly. When a shot landed, it landed hard. Alpha wolves would be slain in a single well-placed shot, and sturdier threats might go down in a small flurry of arrows.

When Silverthorn Keep stood against the rising horde of zombies, these amulets were worn by the keep defenders who unleashed a torrent of arrows into the hungry dead.

Not everyone can wear armour when faced with the hordes of shambling undead. Children and the frail, become targets with little to protect them from gnashing teeth and jaws. In these scenarios magic becomes incredibly important, and these amulets delayed the deaths of countless lives.

Not everyone was happy with such utilitarian approaches to survival, however. As the days drew on and resources grew scarce, empathy began to wane in the population of Emberwood. Amulets of defence were handed out to those determined to have the most need, but the criteria for that became a hot subject of contention.

It would be nice to imagine a world where everyone pulled together to defend against the undead, but human nature sadly shattered that dream. Still, while there would always be those who would attempt to steal the amulets from the needy, there were plenty more who would stand against them.

These amulets now carry an echo of those protectors, shielding its wearers from harm.

Emberwood had its fair share of spellcasters, though lacking the funding of the tower to the south. With more limited resources the mages of Emberwood found other methods to strengthen their art, and the Amulet of Magic was popular among all of them.

Druids worse these amulets while performing rituals around the standing stones, hoping that it might strengthen their bond with nature. The self-proclaimed warlocks, of the Children of Shadows, would sit in darkened basements wearing these glittering amulets as they intoned dark rites that had no effect. The Wild Knight mages would use them more pragmatically, wielding magic against all the threats that the villagers were unaware of.

Might makes right, is the cry of every wannabe despot. Bandos in particular was very fond of this statement. This amulet upholds that notion and does its best to help make sure you are the most correct person around.

It imbues your muscles with body-aligned anima, the magic strengthening the sinew and fortifying your bones. It adds greater force to your swing that will crush your enemies before you.

The goblin general, Snotknucke, wore this amulet and attempted to hunt down Velgar. He believed that, with its magic, he could fell the beast in a single swing of his club. Snotknuckle was stronger, deadlier and more powerful than ever, but this did very little against the rock that Velgar dropped on his head.

No one quite knows why this ring works. It’s as if ancestral grandmothers hover behind the wearer, whispering to them the ancient secrets of their crafting techniques. Old styles, long since lost to tragedy or history, can be restored in a flash of inspiration when someone wears this amulet.

For most, though, they simply find themselves in a trance like state – able to ignore the world around them and focus on their craft. Learning more and more about it with each moment that passes.

This ring is infused with tiny elements of the dragonkin Brakka, a powerful creature and an architect of some skill. His hubris remains, even after his death at Kuldra’s hands, and motes of his knowledge can be preserved within crystalline structures.

Although you cannot glean the secrets of the dragonkin from these motes of memory, his instinct and innate talent for construction and be harnessed to create a ring that makes it easier to understand the art of architecture.

Cooking is a form of magic that few people truly master. Meals can return lost memories, shatter dour moods and rekindle friendships long since abandoned.

This ring was created by accident, when a parent was trying to teach a child how to cook. They were holding the unfinished piece, absent-mindedly, as they explained to their progeny about all the steps needed to understand a recipe.

This knowledge, infused with the emotion of a parents instruction, enchanted the ring so that all future wearers might benefit from their knowledge.

The dwarves know that the earth has a memory, gemstones especially so. What they don’t tell you, is that the earth holds a grudge. Each time a pick pierces the stone or smashes a piece of ore from its home, the earth remembers.

A skilled enchanter can use that grudge, that earthen memory, and turn it back against the grudge-filled earth. Traditionally this is done via jewellery, with dwarven goldsmiths making most of their living from miners purchasing these trinkets.

Ashenfall was the first place that Guthix created runes, but he never passed those secrets on to his followers. It wasn’t until the legendary human, V, discovered the secret, that it was taught to the general population.

Still, something about Ashenfall makes the process easier. No altars are needed, nor talisman’s required, yet runes can be crafted with an abundance that would make the mainland guild’s jealous.

This ring taps into that ease, infusing the soul with some of that ancient knowledge of the island. Be careful sleeping with this on, as you may dream the memories of runecrafters past.

Or of a dog made of cheese. No one knows why.

If trees could move, they would kill us all. That’s the terrible truth that no lumberjack speaks of, instead they silently protect the rest of us from arboreal wrath. When a lumberjack was trained by the druids in Brynmoor, and taught the secrets of the murderous woods, they would be gifted a ring to help them in their battle against the vile trees.

These trinkets have helped lumberjacks defend us all from the cruelty of the woods, in a war that most don’t even believe is happening.

Harold has a job for you and won’t let something as trivial as your expired mortality get in the way. This piece of enchanted jewellery is primed to go off when you’re about to land on Death’s list, whisking you away to safety before the scythe needs to be swung.

There are many sorcerers that have studied these rings for years, going slowly mad as they try to uncover the secrets of immortality. If they could just learn the methods by which this ring operates, then they could extend their lives indefinitely.

Only one wizard ever actually learned this secret, but he forgot the method by which this ring works, for it teleports you at the moment of your death. As the ring does not stop the aging process, this unfortunate mage now spends his eternity in perpetual teleportation – hurled through the abyss, never to take form again.

Hunters have always needed an edge, and this ring is just that. It enhances a hunter’s senses and quietens their movements. Their prey becomes less wary of them, and more vulnerable to attack.

The garou were particularly fond of these rings, and during the few years of peace between the humans of Bramblemead and the garou of Ghornfell, villagers could barter these trinkets for far more than they were worth to other villagers.

Vengeance isn’t a dish best served cold, but one served quickly. Whilst taking years to enact the cruellest retaliation might work in the civilised world, in the wilds of Ashenfall a quick strike is far more valuable.

This ring was first created by one of the Amalgamated, worn as both deterrence and for some small satisfaction when they were targeted by vigilante mobs. It didn’t prevent the bruises, or fix the broken bones, but their assailants attacks would end much more quickly when they discovered they weren’t as untouchable as they thought.

Little is known about the great necromancer, Abraxus, a fact he finds deeply frustrating. In his youth he sought secrets forbidden to others and he wandered the Whispering Swamps of Brynmoor in search of secrets that others dared not know. The swamp was ancient and haunted, so the legends went, and there was a magic to it that was different to any other part of Ashenfall. He explored vaults, caves and battled goblins in the mists, but never found the power that his youth demanded. Until one day, he found the smoke that promised him the power he so desperately craved. 

From there his history is clouded, obscured just as the mist obscured the swamps. He founded a small order of necromancers in Fellhollow and took advantage of the chaos when the Withering began. He sought to bind the souls of the dead into his service, gaining power just as Imaru gained hers from the consumption of the fallen. 

But Abraxus was no dragon, and so frustratingly mortal. A single hoplite spear is all it took to end his life, he would have been embarrassed if he felt it had mattered. As a spirit he lost a lot of things we take for granted, sensation, corporeality, and many of the emotions that everyone else enjoys. Yet, in exchange, he was given the most precious gift of all – time. Time to contemplate. Time to scheme. Time to think about what really matters, in the grand scheme of it all, and he has found his answer. 

His ring, imbued with his dark power, offers no value to him any more. So he bequeaths it to you. In time, perhaps, it may help you become a mage strong enough to earn his respect. Well, a small fraction of it, at least.

For those who wish to keep Zamorak close to their heart, we give you the unholy symbol. To complete the look, we recommend a black top, eyeliner, sticking your tongue out and making Zamorak horns with your hands.

Tanned hide infused with a dragon’s vigor, draconic leather was a gamechanging discovery to the people of Fellhollow. It was as flexible as normal leather, but more resilient than even hardened leather, and it could be fashioned into a variety of shapes useful in a variety of armoured styles.

For a short while, the fashionistas of Emberwood village would use this leather to create garments that evoked the majesty of their draconic protectors, without bearing the scales that might cause undue offence.

Tanned hide infused with a dragon’s vigor, draconic leather was a gamechanging discovery to the people of Fellhollow. It was as flexible as normal leather, but more resilient than even hardened leather, and it could be fashioned into a variety of shapes useful in a variety of armoured styles.

For a short while, the fashionistas of Emberwood village would use this leather to create garments that evoked the majesty of their draconic protectors, without bearing the scales that might cause undue offence.

A discerning archer would do well to craft blue dragonhide leather. The colour is fetching, the defence against magic is second-to-none, and the material has an elasticity that makes it perfect for the nimble stuff that archers tend to do.

If it has a downside, it's a little tight. There's no hiding in blue dragonhide chaps, for example: all the lumps and bumps are out there.

Is it ingenuity of hubris to refine the mighty scales of a dragon down into this thick and sturdy leather?

To the Wild Knights it was the natural order. Hides became leather, which became a resilient second skin they would wear whilst riding into battle. Favoured by archers and scouts due to the nimble flexibility of the scales, whilst still acting as a resilient bulwark against sword, arrow and claw alike.

To the people of Emberwood village, however, such sights were akin to blasphemy. Whilst the green dragons showed little mercy, the villagers loved and adored their majestic draconic protectors, Imaru and Xikotal, and they thought that wearing the skin of their kind might potentially cause offence.

Lord Rasmodel has been put to rest at last.

He rarely knew quiet. His family pushed and cajoled him to further the Rasmodel name. Then he was beset by nightmares, as two malevolent beings grappled over his mind. When the Dreaming Stone shattered, they all took him. He was their spoils. 

We can only hope that, when death came, there was quietus. He felt it for a moment as the Dreaming Stone fell, and - perhaps - he feels it on the journey over the Noumenon. 

The part of him that was a titan - that part remains. You hold it in your hands. Unleash its wrath.

It is uncertain who first crafted the Anti-dragonfire shield. The dwarves claim the honour, as do several human blacksmiths. The goblins tried to take the credit, but their smithing techniques were not nearly refined enough to be believed. The aviansie might have been capable of such a feat, but if they did craft the shield, they never said. 

The aviansie that did visit Ashenfall only stayed in Brynmoor for a short time. They disappeared deeper into the continent, never to be heard from again. 

The Anti-dragonfire shield is a formidable defence, surpassing the strength of iron and capable of withstanding a dragon's lethal breath. Its previous owner, hopeful it could be a weapon against Velgar, was proven wrong. 

Only the most powerful individuals can hope to wield such an object. Yet, even so, it is said that the shield can be improved by combining it with the essence of a dragon, gaining some of the fury of the legendary beast.

Strange magics have transformed the head of a tyrant, into a mystical adornment to this powerful shield. It is still strong, still durable and resists the fury of dragons, but now the shield seethes with Velgar’s wrath.

The magic of the shield is twisted into a bitter poison. One that yearns to strike and rage against any in its path. In battle, it’s possible to turn this poison on one’s enemies, afflicting them with a dragon’s hate.

Sometimes, in the dark, when the night is silent, it’s as if the shield whispers angry curses into the darkness. As if the last vestiges of Velgar’s fury are carried on the wind as hateful poison.

A shield with the visage of Imaru the devourer welded to the front. Holding the shield feels cold to the touch and instils within you a hunger for battle. Your blood pumps faster with a terrible need to rend your enemy limb from limb.

While metal may be the preferred choice of weapon makers, the granite maul stands as a testament to the raw power of stone. This legendary weapon, revered among hammer wielders, is nothing more than a massive, heavy stone affixed to a stick. Yet, in the hands of a skilled wielder, it becomes a force of unparalleled destruction. 

It was developed by ancient dwarves long ago after one dwarf got fed up with the rock hammer he was using to fight a nest of gargoyles. The beasts were strong and numerous, and their skin was almost impenetrable to conventional weaponry; only rock hammers seemed to hurt them. In frustration, one dwarf took the biggest hunk of granite he could find and turned it into a legend. 

Since that moment, the weapon has been refined, using various special components to make it more wieldy in combat. The original creator having done himself an injury with the recoil.

Crystal bows are not crafted but sung into existence. Harmonies are layered over one another to teach the weapon a story so that it might understand why it needs to be strong. Traditionally, the plight of the elves, their loneliness without their goddess, and the many dangers they must face are sung. 

The shimmering substance they’re made from is traditionally a shard of the elven goddess Seren, left behind when her crystal form shattered at the end of the Third Age God Wars. 

This bow isn’t the same material, and the song is different, too. There’s no divine essence in the crystal, no lament of the elves. Instead, it is sung memories of a lifetime together, distilled down into facets reflecting in the crystalline flaws. In the moonlight, those facets sparkle like tiny stars held within glass—moments of hardship, conflict, happiness, and grief. 

It is music as a weapon, with memory as the handle and sorrow as the string.

In the third age, before someone could wield a staff of light, it would first need to be blessed by three people. 

A priest would bless it first, instilling the weapon with Saradomin’s wisdom. A monk would bless it second, imbuing the staff with the humility of Saradomin. Finally, an icyene general would give their blessing, dedicating the weapon to Saradomin’s righteousness. 

Those battlemages chosen to wield such a sacred weapon would dive into battle against Zamorak’s forces and believe themselves invulnerable. 

They were often proved wrong. 

Yet, those who carried the staff into battle seemed to survive longer than their compatriots. The defensive magic of the staff kept them alive longer than others. A demon once stole a staff and tried to use that power for himself. Still, when he tried to channel demonic power through the staff, it is said that the explosion could be seen from across the battlefield.

There are myths and anecdotes about Zamorak appearing to the inhabitants of Dowdun Reach. Few truly believe them. Zamorak is not one for hiding his presence: he's more theatrical than that.

It may be hard to believe, but some have tried to 'summon' Zamorak to Dowdun Reach. Call it the hubris of a demon summoner: a student feels they have mastered the art of summoning demons, so believes the next rung on the ladder is god. Never mind that Zamorak has no ounce of demon within him.

Zamorak can be accused of many things, but he does have a sense of humour. These attempts have been greeted with a gift. He has been known to kick a bloodveld through the portal, or another creature he has to hand. It gives the demon summoner a little spot-test.

The abyssal demon is a foul beast. A single one can massacre a village before the warning bell can even sound. Crafting a weapon from their remains symbolizes triumph against adversity or perhaps just hubris in its purest form. 

Infused with the hate of the demon, each whip crack generates unbearable pain. Victims find themselves overcome with suffering as the agony seeps into the wounds the whips leave behind. 

It is said that the whip even feeds on that misery, growing stronger with each strike, each life taken. The hate imbues the weapon with a desire to inflict more pain, and it grows stronger each time such a wish is granted. Or, so the legends say. 

Only one Wild Knight has ever chosen to wield the whip in vengeance against the demon for a fallen sibling. Her name is dust in the wind of history, but there are still creatures that hide in the shadows, afraid of the crack of her whip.

A common misconception is that a farming potion is poured onto your crops, rather than into your mouth. While it might sound sensible - it's the crops that are growing, not you - it fundamentally misunderstands what the farming potion does.

The farming potion increases the experience you receive when you farm. It achieves this by making you sensitive to the wants and needs of your plants. You become intensely aware of them. You will know when even a single aphid lands on a leaf; when the last drop has been siphoned from the soil; when a potato wants to hear you sing. You are attuned to your crops, to the point that you might even find kinship with them. If you begin sleeping in your cabbage patch, you should consider lowering your consumption.

You often hear stories about great anglers who have that one marine animal they've never caught. A whale perhaps, or a darting salmon. Well, I had a friend once who had an enmity with a prawn. It was a big prawn, mind you, and every time he caught it, it would leap out of his net and get away. He accused that pawn of gloating: he believed it waited until he hauled in the net before scarpering.

It got to the point where my friend traded his week's wages for a fishing potion. Not a lesser or weak fishing potion: a third-tier, freshly made fishing potion. He was going to catch that prawn, even if he had to starve for days. 

But the potion didn't make him any better at catching the prawn. Instead, he became attuned to the prawn. He was so aware that he knew what it was thinking. Now, every time the prawn escapes, my friend can hear it cackle and let out a little "doofus!".

While the weaker variant of antipoison is sufficient for everyday usage, there are some situations that require a more potent resistance. The toxic mists in Ghornfell, over the swamps, are more powerful than a weaker resistance will protect against. These lesser potions strengthen the immune system and keep the toxic miasma from the drinker’s blood. Imperfect, still, but a much greater chance of survival.

Just because you're protected by antipoison, it doesn't mean that you should start glugging poison or poking yourself with poison-tipped arrows. Your body still needs to get rid of that poison, and it's not pleasant on the exit.

While the antifire potion is a miraculous thing - one of the most useful items on Ashenfall - it does have a flaw. It can be hard to know when its effect wears off. Knowing that you are protected, or not protected, while standing in front of a dragon is something of a problem.

One solution is to bring someone with you. Get them to count while you fight a dragon. Then they can give you a happy thumbs-up while protected and a sad thumbs-down when you're not. 

Of course, they probably need antifire if they're going to assume that role. Which means a third-party to give a thumbs-up or thumbs-down for that second person. Yeah, we see the flaw in the plan.

Poison has always been a problem for hunters. The forests are full of venomous creatures, just waiting for a chance to strike. The difference between an edible mushroom and one filled with deadly toxins, is hard to spot on a moonless night. Thus the antipoison potion was once of the first to be created. A glorious catch-all that fought against all the venoms and toxins of the realm.

They’re also surprisingly good for a hangover.

Looking and smelling suspiciously like coffee, this potion lets crafters sit down and create their favourite things. Tapestries, wood carvings, anything that requires time and focus, can be enhanced with the focus this potion provides.

Looking and smelling suspiciously like coffee, this potion lets crafters sit down and create their favourite things. Tapestries, wood carvings, anything that requires time and focus, can be enhanced with the focus this potion provides.

This potion streamlines your vision and hones your reflexes. Your synapses surge with clarity and you find it easier to understand the nuance of the blade.

The Wild Knights relied heavily on these potions to train their new fighters during the decline of the Guthixian faith. With fewer numbers, it became more important to teach people quickly.

This potion is sweet, yet strong. The ingredients have stewed within the mixture for some time and allowed it to increase in potency. While it’s perfectly fine to drink it directly from the bottle, you find yourself wishing you had a nice mug to use instead.

Still, this potion makes you acutely (haha, see what I did there?) aware of angles. You can measure a plank of wood with just a few well placed glares. The inner architect in you revels at the new possibilities before you, and you find yourself planning much further ahead.

It’s easy to forget that cooking is more than just throwing ingredients into the pot and applying fire. There are balances of flavours. Subtle blendings of ingredients to create that perfect mix. This potion assists with that by enhancing the smell and tastebuds of the chef, so they can learn just how much of an ingredient needs to be added for optimal results.

This potion assists with that by enhancing the smell and tastebuds of the chef, so they can learn just how much of an ingredient needs to be added for optimal results.

This potion makes it easier to swing a pickaxe. Your muscles are more relaxed and able to handle greater strain, making it easier to strike at the ore, learning the weak points.

Druids used to whip up huge batches of this for visiting dwarves, who would mix it with beer for dubious results.

Ashenfall is the birthplace of runecrafting. When Guthix settled here, he discovered the strange anima pouring out of the ground, different strands of magic resonating with certain concepts and natural laws.

This potion lets you see the world as he did, just for a while. You can spot the colourful flows of anima as they shimmer in the air and you learn how better to trap them in the rune essence, creating the potential for future magic.

Woodcutting is less about speed and strength, and more about precision. The focus this potion provides, lets you hone your senses, attacking the same place repeatedly along the tree trunk. The result is more efficiently felled trees and far fewer splinters.

Woodcutting is less about speed and strength, and more about precision. The focus this potion provides, lets you hone your senses, attacking the same place repeatedly along the tree trunk. The result is more efficiently felled trees and far fewer splinters.

Woodcutting is less about speed and strength, and more about precision. The focus this potion provides, lets you hone your senses, attacking the same place repeatedly along the tree trunk. The result is more efficiently felled trees and far fewer splinters.

Hold a healing potion in your hands and doubt might enter the mind. Do you drink it? Do you apply it directly to the wound? Is it a suppository? Well, allow us to clarify: it is taken orally. 

A healing potion won't just address your egregious malady. It will help with minor nuisances, too. If you've got a spot on the inside of your nostril, it will tidy that up nicely. An armpit itch? Gone in seconds. Ingrowing toenail? Consider it done.

No one is entirely sure who invented this potion, but those without the knowledge were invariably the losers in warfare. In ancient times armies would be given doses of this potion before they were sent off to battle. It made them deadlier, faster and vastly increased their chances of victory.

The Children of Shadows used to try brew large quantities of this potion every time they went off on a mission, so that they would have the edge were they ever caught. Many a guard could attest to the power of this potion, but fortunately it didn’t make up for a general lack of melee skill.

A step up from the weaker version, this potion allows the body to absorb more of the ambient anima, which in turn ramps up the healing. The potion allows the drinker to shrug off greater physical trauma than before.

For years herblore masters have worked on versions that might work on emotional trauma, but to these days there has been little success.

Although humans cannot use magic without runes, they can become quite proficient conduits for power. A wizard and a farmer, when possessing the same runes, do not produce the same results, the former far outshines the former. 

This made some farmers pretty upset. For years they had ranted to their cows and sheep about how wizards were just snooty uppity types with shiny rocks. Yet, faced with this revelation, there was no denying that a farmer and a wizard simply weren’t equals in disguise. 

One farmer wanted to level the playing field and brought in a local witch who was had a grudge against the snooty men in blue robes. 

Between the two of them, they found a way to improve a person’s connection to the magic within them via a magical potion. Those who drank it cast spells more efficiently and with greater skill than they ever could before. 

The farmer wanted to use this new advantage to put the wizards in their place, raze down the tower and claim magic a tool of the people. Unfortunately, the witch saw a different future, one that sparkled with glittering gold coins. 

She died happily, wealthier than she had ever dreamed, surrounded by adoring young suiters who wanted a piece of her inheritance. The farmer died after being trampled to death by cows that were tired of his grumbling.

This potions helps you sleep all night and work all day. It gives you the feeling of well oiled elbow and shoulder joints, and it numbs the pain in your muscles that’s created from swinging an axe for long stretches at a time.

This potions helps you sleep all night and work all day. It gives you the feeling of well oiled elbow and shoulder joints, and it numbs the pain in your muscles that’s created from swinging an axe for long stretches at a time.

This potions helps you sleep all night and work all day. It gives you the feeling of well oiled elbow and shoulder joints, and it numbs the pain in your muscles that’s created from swinging an axe for long stretches at a time.

Mining is a strenuous task. Each swing of that pickaxe makes your muscles ache and your bones quiver. Exhaustion creeps into your soul and takes root, making it take longer and longer to gather that precious ore.

This potion acts as a pick-me-up. An energising drink that makes you a monster of a miner, able to swing your pickaxe as though it barely weighed anything at all.

Mining is a strenuous task. Each swing of that pickaxe makes your muscles ache and your bones quiver. Exhaustion creeps into your soul and takes root, making it take longer and longer to gather that precious ore.

This potion acts as a pick-me-up. An energising drink that makes you a monster of a miner, able to swing your pickaxe as though it barely weighed anything at all.

As with many physical activities, you can over-exert yourself when mining. Lactic acid builds up in the muscles, sweat clouds the vision, and an accidental pickaxe swing can lodge in the head. A mining potion addresses all of these shortcomings. Lactic acid and sweat forms slowly. The likelihood of accidents is reduced.

What it can't do is improve the quality or amount of ore. Pour it in your mouth or pour it on the deposit: it won't matter. No mining potion is going to help you there.

Alchemists have long strived to create potions to rid mankind of all their ills. Normally these potions are regeneratives, that boost the body’s ability to repair itself by drawing in the ambient anima and infusing it into the blood and tissue of the drinker. On the mainland this changes healing from a matter of days, to a matter of hours. On Ashenfall the results are far more pronounced.

The ambient anima of the region is so strong, that wounds heal in moments. Cuts vanish as if they never were, the wounds healing so quickly that even scars cannot form. The weaker potions burn themselves out before they can heal massive injuries, but the results are impressive none the less.
Tacky like your great aunt’s wardrobe, this sticky substance has a wide range of uses when it comes to crafting.

Congratulations on killing enough Black Knights to form a bar from their resulting scraps. Don't think about the children you have orphaned, the dreams that you have impaled on the sharp end of a greatsword. Don't think about the concentrated misery you hold in your hands. 

It is rather shiny, though, is it not? Black Metal, particularly if you have the draconic sort (more chromatic, less matte), is mesmerising.
Blue concentrated and refined, melted down and reforged into a cuboid shape so that you may carry around purest blue with you wherever you go.
An ingot of a copper-tin alloy. Highly prized by blacksmiths.

Forged from gold ore, this glistening block of metal has been the cause of many conflicts over the ages. Stockpiles of them have been instrumental in dwarven civil wars and there are still plenty of people today who would gladly slit their sibling’s throat, just for a single bar.
A heavy ingot of iron.

In the Third Age on Ashenfall, mithril was incredibly desirable. It was only available in small quantities then, as its scarcity - perhaps inevitably - made it the fashion of the time. High-priests would incorporate it into their rosaries. Community leaders would wear it as brooches. It was rare enough that people would comment when they saw it. "Ooh, Rosie's doing well for herself". That sort of thing. 

The idea of a mithril bar would have been mind-boggling to anyone in the Third Age. No matter their alignment, they'd have stabbed you in the belly and run away cackling with it. But here you are with an entire ingot. Take a moment and consider yourself a Third Age king.
Forged from silver ore, this glittering cuboid is highly prized by jewellers.

If iron had been a marvel, steel was a revelation. Sturdier structures. More durable armour. Weapons sharper and stronger than ever seen before. If the people of Brynmoor had known about steel, then perhaps the garou threat would have been over almost as soon as it began.

For the people of Emberwood, it led to superior farming tools, stronger buildings and better hunting equipment. After all, what need did people have for weapons and armour with a dragon protecting them?
Did you know that someone who fires a crossbow is often considered an 'arbalister' or 'arbalist'? Don't say that these journals don't teach you something.
A block of stone... there's not really much more to say here.

Not all cats are terrified of crossbows, though most of the Bramblemead population quickly learned to fear them. Some cats could see their merit but were painfully aware of their human pet’s limited imagination.

Mittens was the lifelong owner of Gothren Actillian. A blacksmith’s cat, he had learned how to keep the forge fires perfectly hot by playfully jumping on the bellows periodically – something his pet seemed to enjoy. He knew the right length of time a sword should be cooled for and would make sure his human stuck to that timing by offering useful alarms made of glorious clattering things.

But blurite? That was a new one. Mittens could see there was little value to the metal, beyond some weird human aesthetic that he did not understand, yet there was some potential to the construction. With carefully timed meows, and some creative knocking over of reagents, Mittens was able to engineer a crossbow of superior design, worthy of his cherished pet.

The walking dead are strange things, animated by hunger, need, rage and a touch of magic. In Ashenfall it is the hunger of Imaru that drives them, as her great maw draws in the souls of both the living and the dead, the afflicted try to cling to life with everything that they have.

They fail, but the drive remains. Fear, desperation, and simple need keeps the body moving, if not alive. This changes the body, that hunger and rot seeping into the very bones and changing them to be something other than they were.

When ground down, these bones become a powder containing hints of that animating magic. A necromantic resonance with a wide range of uses.

The complicated mechanisms of the crossbow sparked a small technological revolution in Bramblemead. Such devices had once been the domain of the dwarves, who guarded their advanced techniques to maintain their smithing dominion. However, soon human smiths found themselves creating these complex trigger mechanisms.

There were casualties, certainly. Some mechanisms tightened strings too greatly, snapping back with a force that could cause serious harm.

One notable example involved the pet belonging to the village priest, a curious feline with absolutely no sense of self-preservation. The cat entered the workshop, as she so often did, and took it upon herself to knock everything she could to the floor. Unfortunately, in doing so, she triggered the release mechanism being worked on and quickly learned that cats are not meant to fly.

What’s better than a dead tree? A dead tree that has been burned to the point of carbonisation. No longer do you see unsightly green leaves, but now the wonderful blackness of charcoal. Highly praised by smiths. Way better than stupid old logs of wood.  

This journal entry was sponsored by the tree haters of Lumbridge, if you’re interested in joining their focus group, please speak to Tim near Bob’s Axes.

Much prettier now that it’s not leaving nasty red stains everywhere.
This clay nicknack will look really nice on a shelf, once you’ve cooked it in the kiln. Otherwise it’ll just leave red stains everywhere.
This mould can be used in crafting.
Once cooked, this will serve as a useful template for further crafting projects.
This vessel can be sealed to allow for the storage of potions.
A sticky clay container in need of a kiln.

More elegant and refined than cotton cloth, this fabric was very popular mong Emberwood citizenry. They would craft beautiful dresses, fine shirts and elegant robes, many of which included patterns of undulating blue and black in honour of the serpentine dances they would watch in the sky, as Imaru and Xikotal swam the air currents together.
Bolts of sturdy linen cloth, excellent for crafting magical armour with.

It is almost imperceptible, but mystic cloth hums. It's the magical resonance: the elemental threads pushing against each other as their charge shifts. That oscillation also means that there is a lightly massaging effect on the wearer. It's entirely possible to feel calm from the vibrations as you stride into battle.

You might also be mistaken for thinking that mystic cloth is blue. On a microscopic level, it's actually a multitude of colours, combining to form the illusion of blue. As a magician once told me, it's the same effect as a blue sky: it is the result of all the colours in the world combining to make wondrous azure.
A bolt of cloth that has been reinforced. Makes for sturdier armour than normal cloth.
Bolts of durable woolen cloth, popular with mages and anyone that likes a little thermal insulation in their armour.

Following certain incidents with cats and crossbow limbs, a superstition began to build in Bramblemead about the danger of crossbows and felines. It was considered bad luck to house a cat in the same home as a crossbow, with reasoning ranging from the safety of the cat, to the safety of the human population should the cats rise up and develop opposable thumbs.

Whilst harmless at first, when the need for crossbows grew due to the conflict with the garou, cats found themselves with fewer and fewer homes willing to house them. Over the years this led to domestic cats being a rare sight in Bramblemead.

It also created a deep-seated resentment in the cats, and they plotted their revenge. Most would tell you that cats had nothing to do with the destruction of Bramblemead and the Brynmoor settlements, yet if you ask a cat, they will simply purr with a sinister grin on their face.

There are many theories on what ectoplasm is, each as disturbing as the last. Some say that it’s emotion, condensed over years of existence. Others think that it’s some sort of infection of the soul, as only the undead seem to produce it.

In truth, ectoplasm is none of these. It’s the outer shell of a soul, the skin more or less, and acts as a sort of lubrication between the flesh and the spirit. Tiny bits of reality combine with the immaterial otherness of the underworld and congeal into a sort of spiritual mixture that ghosts and other undead find incredible useful.

Necromancers prize the stuff, finding a myriad of uses for it. Most people find it weird and decidedly “icky”.

Used to craft Fire arrows.

Some people just like to watch the world burn… and you can too, with these new handy fire oils! That’s right, just lob one of these beauties at your enemies and watch them ignite. Enjoy their screams as their skin boils away and breathe in the delightful aroma of a fresh barbecue!

Fire oil, like snake oil, was once the creation of a travelling merchant to Ashenfall. She had journeyed to Brynmoor alongside some of the Saradominist settlers, and with her she brought pungent unguents, elucidating elixirs, marvellous medicines, and weapons of localised destruction.

When her wares summoned the call for refunds, she would dazzle the onlookers with a sudden fiery display and then disappear into the alleyways until she had been forgotten. She would then return in one of a staggering array of disguises, until the tragic day when her wagon hit a pothole during a quick getaway and her many, many, bottles of fire oils decided to sing the song of their people.

Fortunately, even though she and everything she owned, tragically found itself reduced to smouldering atoms, her recipe lived on and found more conventional uses.

Let's take a moment to recognise your glass-blowing skills. 

...

Congratulations on your craftmanship. You have the puff of an angel.

Beautiful, yet hard to handle, the creation of molten glass was a turning point for Gielinorian civilization. It allowed the creation of vials, windows, and those pretty little animals that grandmothers love to collect and put on shelves.
When you take me out of a window, you leave a grieving wife. When you stick me in a door, you will save somebody’s life. What am I?

The glass vessel. The emblem of potential. It could find itself filled with anything. Water, demonic oil, maple syrup. It could smash before it ever sees use. It could languish in someone's inventory, forgotten for months. In many ways, we are all glass vessels. 

Check back in your journal for other delightful moments of poignancy and philosophy. You may find your life unutterably changed by the wisdom there.
By grinding clay down into powder, you can create a much smoother medium for crafting. The grinding process gets rid of various impurities and allows for more resilient results.

It takes a lot of effort to crush granite into powder. Blood, sweat and tears go into the process of refining this solid stone into something more manageable. In doing so you prove yourself a master over the earth itself, though the earth rarely acknowledges your claim.

Clearly, all limestone is ground limestone, as it comes from the ground. In many places it IS the ground. But this is specifically limestone that has been ground down into a dust. 

Ground limestone's primary use is in glassblowing. Should you have the crafting capability and a healthy, strong puff, you can use the ground limestone to make molten glass and then glass-produce.
Sometimes you just want to prove how much better you are than a lump of rock, by grinding it down into a fine powder. Yeah, take that sandstone, you’re not all that tough as dust, are you?

Blue dragon dust is one of those things where you wonder who invented it, and what curious sequence of events led to it happening. Some unknown person had the wherewithal to strip a dragon of dragon scales, crush those scales in a pestle and mortar, then apply the resulting dust to a potion. Not only that, they downed said potion and stood in the path of a dragon. Does that not seem inexorably mad to you? 

But thank you, inexorably mad person, because now we have this potent ingredient which can protect a person from dragonfire. You have been forgotten in time, but your blue dusty legacy lives on.

Much harder to stub your foot on in powdered form. Stone ground down and down until all that remains is a fine powder useful in ways that big lumps of stone are not. Like cake frosting for your enemies.
Fun fact, these tiny granules of wheat are surprisingly combustible. So be careful with fire magic around them.

Following certain incidents with cats and crossbow limbs, a superstition began to build in Bramblemead about the danger of crossbows and felines. It was considered bad luck to house a cat in the same home as a crossbow, with reasoning ranging from the safety of the cat, to the safety of the human population should the cats rise up and develop opposable thumbs.

Whilst harmless at first, when the need for crossbows grew due to the conflict with the garou, cats found themselves with fewer and fewer homes willing to house them. Over the years this led to domestic cats being a rare sight in Bramblemead.

It also created a deep-seated resentment in the cats, and they plotted their revenge. Most would tell you that cats had nothing to do with the destruction of Bramblemead and the Brynmoor settlements, yet if you ask a cat, they will simply purr with a sinister grin on their face.

You may wonder where the 'cursed' moniker comes from. There is no need to worry: you're not cursed by wearing it, and you're not going to be visited by the three dragons of past, present and future in the night for making it. It has a wildly different etymology. 

The 'cursed' is a tannery thing, you see. Cursed leather is notoriously hard to work and shape. Only the foremost tanners can manipulate it. But even they have a hard time, often exclaiming "cursed leather!" at the top of their lungs.

The tanned hide of a powerful dire wolf. It's sturdy, thick, ready for enchantment and more resilient than normal leather. 

A legend speaks of a warrior that wore the tanned hide of a dire wolf. He wore it proudly as he walked the streets and used it to strike fear into the hearts of bandits and criminals alike. After all, a warrior that could best such a beast, was no one to be trifled with. 

The story goes that one day he met another warrior, proud, skillful and handsome, and the two of them became the best of friends. They would fight together, spar together and rescued helpless villagers as an unstoppable pair. Until the night of the full moon, where the truth was revealed. Where once his new friend had stood, there was now a dread wolf of midnight-black fur and eyes of burning coal. They fought again, but gone was the jovial sparring of friends. Now they fought as man and beast, tooth and claw versus blade and shield, until all that was left of the two of them was this story.

Tanned animal hide has a distinctive smell and was surprisingly popular among some of the quieter villagers of Bramblemead. This versatile material can be used to craft a wide range of items, including armour and weapons.

Tanned animal hide has a distinctive smell and was surprisingly popular among some of the quieter villagers of Bramblemead. This versatile material can be used to craft a wide range of items, including armour and weapons.
Thicker and more resilient than normal leather. Still smells though.

What do you call a crossbow without limbs? A REALLY cross bow. 

Oh shush, you. That joke plays well at fletching conventions.

Distilled from Swamp Tar and Charcoal.

Once known as “thick water”, naptha is liquid fire in waiting. It takes little to bring this substance to ignition, even too bright a sunny day can bring the raging flame from within.

As such, this material is widely sought by anyone with a strong desire to set things aflame. Pyromaniacs, alchemists and dragon cultists alike yearn for this liquid.

Combine corrupt bonemeal and vile ashes and the result is demonic oil. We should be specific about what kind of oil this is: please do no pour it into a frying pan or use it on a squeaky hinge. This is potent oil, as the 'demonic' prefix will attest. It is used for crafting, and solely for crafting.

Now you've got us wondering what would happen if you did actually add it to a frying pan or a squeaky hinge. Obviously, please don't do it. But if you do happen to do it, through an accident or momentary lack of wisdom, could you let us know what happens?

Although the recipe is significantly different to the oil created in Morytania, this oil serves a similar purpose. The priests of Emberwood created this oil to assist in funerary rites, the strange scent of the oil would help the dead pass on when cremated and, crucially, in the time of the Withering, the bodies would be reduced to ash, unable to get up and cause further trouble.

Originally this oil used pressed olives, and the influence of the gods to create an oil that would burn a body sufficiently to avoid the dead rising. As the Withering increased and civilisation began to fall into ruin, the priests found alternative methods to create a sufficiently strong flame.

Steel crossbow limbs are sturdier than their counterparts, with a greater durability that allows them to create stronger and more dangerous crossbows.

Indeed, this durability seems to have put a nominal end to the unfortunate curse between cats and crossbow limb related catastrophes.

As a result, in Fellhollow, cats still had a place in the home, even after they had been systematically outcast from Bramblemead. It was said that Xikotal, in particular, enjoyed the gentle purring noises that they made and would surround his lair with hundreds of the creatures so that he might be lulled into a gentle sleep.

Because “corpse thread” is far too sinister a title, this thread is referred to as fine thread. It’s stronger, thinner and more malleable than cotton threads. The villagers of Emberwood enjoyed this thread in their clothing, creating garments that would suffer greater strain than their previous gear. Farmers were particularly fond of it.
Woven strands that create a more durable thread. Useful in a wide range of crafting projects.
Woven strands that create a more durable thread. Useful in a wide range of crafting projects.

There are many steps to making mystic robes - fibres to thread, thread to cloth, cloth to robes - but you would be wise not to skip any of them. Habberdash the Impatient, a magician who lived in Temple Woods, attempted to make his robes solely from thread. He would wrap himself, round and round, with spool after spool of mystic thread, and then attempt battle.

You can imagine how it went. He got snagged on a goblin's helmet and quickly unwound, mid-battle. We're not sure who would have been more shocked: Habberdash, who promptly got stabbed in the gut, or the goblin, who was confronted with a wizard in his undergarments.

Woven from swamp weed, this thread is both stronger than it appears and magically stable. Enchantments woven into garments, crafted with this thread, are more potent than those woven with normal fibres.

The fluffy coats from the fluffiest creatures, refined and wound down into a single thread. Useful for crafting various garments and highly prized by grandmothers everywhere.

On the mainland, the most impressive collection of wool belongs to Great-Aunt Potterington, in Kandarin. Her sister, Granny Potterington, is constantly trying to claim this title for herself and has accelerated her sheep breeding program in an attempt to mass produce as much wool as possible.

JOURNAL_Recipes_Resources_Thread_Wool_Fr.displayName: Wool Thread From Soft Animal Fur
descriptionPages:
The fluffy coats from the fluffiest creatures, refined and wound down into a single thread. Useful for crafting various garments and highly prized by grandmothers everywhere.

On the mainland, the most impressive collection of wool belongs to Great-Aunt Potterington, in Kandarin. Her sister, Granny Potterington, is constantly trying to claim this title for herself and has accelerated her sheep breeding program in an attempt to mass produce as much wool as possible.

Used to craft Barbed Arrows or to coat your equipped melee weapon with barbs, causing Bleed buildup for a short duration.

Sometimes you just really want to hurt something. It happens. We each have that little voice that tells us to make something bleed, just a little.

Well, blacksmiths thought to themselves “what if we listen to that voice?” and created these little doohickies. Barbs for your weapons, so that they may tear, rend and otherwise force your opponents to bleed with every strike.

Used in crafting Poison Arrows or to coat your equipped Melee Weapon with Poison for a short time.

There are times in life where you really want someone to regret ever meeting you. It could be a long-held grudge, it could be on the battlefield, or it could be in a never-ending meeting that could so easily have been an email but has now persisted past your lunchbreak and you had so been looking forward to that sushi… ahem… sorry, where was I? Oh, yes, pain!

Poison is sometimes considered to be a coward’s weapon. Personally, I prefer to think of it as the smart weapon choice, after all, honour is only worth anything if you’re the winner of the battle. A corpse with honour still rots in the dirt. With a blade coated in poison, you can almost guarantee the death of your enemies, all it takes is a single nick and their fate is sealed.

An investment well worth making, if you ask me.

Magic is made up of motes of anima, refined and collected into specific forms. Rune essence can be made to hold these motes, creating dedicated magical components for spells and enchantments. Wild anima is useful for magical construction, but cannot be converted into true spellcraft, as the motes and unaligned and prone to strange reactions.

Rune essence has the unique property of absorbing and transforming ambient magic into something that can be used by even races without a natural magical aptitude, like humans.

An air rune contains anima that is aligned with the wind itself. A practitioner of magic can take the rune and convert it into a gentle breeze. Or, with enough of them, a gale force wind.

Very popular in the hot summer months.

Astral runes borrow resonance from the stars, that silent hum when looking up at the night sky. They embody the power of distance and displacement, action from inaction, movement in the dark. Astral runes are used primarily in spells designed to summon an object to the caster’s location, making it a very popular rune with the lazier magicians.

Imbued with the harmony of soil and dirt, the earth rune stands firm in a world of mercurial change. Predominantly used to manipulate or control natural elements associated with, soil and rock, it can also be used to reference the permanence of things.

Guthix created runes in the first age, here on Ashenfall. He found a way of capturing the ambient wild anima, of the continent, in the floating rune essence around the region. He would then give these runes to his druids, teaching them the fundamentals of magic.

Fire runes contain enough magical fire to light a campfire. Battlemages prize them for the ability to lob that small fire at an enemy instead. Either way, they’re a very handy resource.
Little scraps of hide, snout, claw and fur. Most people would toss it away in disgust, but you - you stick it in your pockets for a rainy day.

More and more of the afflicted began talking in the voice that Rasmodel recognised: the voice from his recurring dreams. They were a chorus, moved from the land of dreams to his waking nightmare. Doubt burned in him. Was this epidemic his fault? Was it here for him? 

He couldn't hide his relationship to the epidemic anymore. When he walked past the afflicted, they would move from incoherent to coherent. They would sit up and speak to him. The message was the same: "submit to the Dreaming Stone". There were variations on that theme: use the Dreaming Stone; spread its energy; offer your mind to it.

Lod Rasmodel was resolved. He would never submit. Perhaps therein was the trap. Perhaps it was shrewd psychology to make him destroy the stone. 

But destroy it he did. All it took was a single blow from his warhammer. A great and savage arc. As the crystal shattered and the shards punctured his skin, he felt peace. The voices were silenced. he knelt, bleeding and happy. 

And then the aftershocks came.

While heavier than other materials, there’s little argument that a bronze shield is a superior defence to wood and leather materials. The metal turns aside almost any blow, protecting the wielder behind its mass. 

Even the Wild Knights, who usually shunned metal weapons, would don bronze shields into battle. Their philosophy was that a life is worth more than a principle.
